all_findings.finding: Query communities — maximal sets of queries where all pairs are related — can be reconstructed from sparse search-log graphs by densifying the graph (adding edges proportional to shared neighbors) and then clustering, enabling early detection of commercial intent before a user appears in a shopping vertical.
all_findings.implication: This provides a formal target construct (query community) for predicting downstream purchase behavior from pre-commercial search signals, which could be adapted to detect emerging consumer needs or interests before they manifest as product searches.
all_findings.open_question: How should query communities be maintained over time as interests and search behavior drift? Does the same approach work in domains with very short or very long purchase funnels?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Query communities extracted from search logs in a given vertical (e.g., outdoor gear) will increase recall of commercial intent predictions by at least 50% over individual query co-occurrence baselines.

all_findings.finding: Two complementary recommendation methods (co-occurrence with temporal ordering and hitting-set explanation) both benefit from query communities: communities increase the number of recommendations from 9K to 11K (co-occurrence) and 36K to 42K (hitting set), while shifting the quality distribution from mostly Obvious (54% → 11%) toward Good and Surprising (30% → 61%).
all_findings.implication: Aggregating search behavior at the community level (rather than individual query level) amplifies weak signals and uncovers non-obvious product relationships, suggesting that interest-based grouping is a viable strategy for long-range recommendation without requiring purchase or rating data.
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal community granularity — too coarse aggregates noise, too fine loses signal? Can community-based recommendations generalize across different time horizons and user segments?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A recommendation system using co-occurrence counts aggregated over query communities will achieve higher precision and recall for long-range commercial intent than one using raw query-level co-occurrence, when evaluated on held-out search log data.

all_findings.finding: The densification step (adding edges between query pairs with probability proportional to their Dice coefficient of shared neighbors) increases the number of discovered communities by 4× compared to clustering the raw sparse graph, while maintaining that only 2.6% of discovered communities are 'Bad' and 69% are 'Interesting' or 'Surprising' vs. 0% for query-click graph clusters.
all_findings.implication: Densification is an effective and theoretically grounded preprocessing step that recovers latent relationships in sparse interaction graphs. This could be applied to any sparse co-occurrence or interaction graph (e.g., product co-view, search-to-click) to discover latent groupings.
all_findings.open_question: How sensitive is the optimal densification probability to graph density, cluster overlap, and degree distribution? Can the Dice-coefficient heuristic be replaced with learned edge probabilities?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Applying the densification-then-clustering pipeline to e-commerce co-view graphs will yield product clusters that are more diverse and less obvious than clusters from the raw co-view graph, measured by human judgment of surprisingness.

all_findings.finding: A seq2seq neural architecture that models 'information deficit' — the semantic gap between what a user has seen (landing page content proxied by URLs) and what they need — improves query word retention prediction accuracy by 9.22% (AOL) and 5.88% (Yandex) over majority-class baselines, and improves F1 for the removal class by 4.45–4.87% over the state-of-the-art FMN model.
all_findings.implication: Explicitly modeling the deficit between user information need and provided results is an effective inductive bias for understanding query reformulation. This suggests that search engines could predict which parts of a query the user will keep/drop, enabling real-time result diversification.
all_findings.open_question: How should 'information deficit' be computed when landing page content is unavailable or anonymized? Can the deficit signal be integrated into ranking or query suggestion in an online setting?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A search engine that diversifies results based on predicted word retention (from the information deficit model) will reduce session length and increase user satisfaction compared to a standard ranking baseline.

all_findings.finding: The same information deficit encoder applied to next-query selection (K=20 candidates) improves MRR over FMN from 0.853→0.864 (AOL) and 0.686→0.693 (Yandex), both statistically significant (p<0.001). The improvement comes from modeling the difference between past-query embeddings and past-click embeddings as an explicit deficit signal, rather than treating them as separate inputs.
all_findings.implication: The deficit-based approach provides consistent, albeit modest, gains across two demographically different search log datasets (US from 2006 and Russia from 2013), suggesting the mechanism of information deficit is domain-agnostic and generalizes across time and culture.
all_findings.open_question: Can explicit deficit modeling benefit open-ended next-query generation (not just selection from K candidates) and longer-term session prediction beyond the immediate next query?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: An information-deficit augmented query suggestion model will yield higher relevance gains in sessions with more query reformulations (higher information deficit) than in sessions where the first query already satisfies the need.

all_findings.finding: The dataset includes ~3.6M queries from 1.5M sessions (AOL) and ~11M queries from 6.6M sessions (Yandex). The model processes past queries, past clicks (URLs encoded at character level via GRU), and current query through separate encoders, combining them using element-wise operations to predict word-level retention or removal.
all_findings.implication: Character-level URL encoding is a viable proxy for landing page content when page text is unavailable, allowing privacy-preserving session modeling. The data distribution across two large real-world logs provides a strong benchmark for query reformulation research.
all_findings.open_question: How much additional signal would be gained from actual landing page content (when available) compared to URL-only proxies? What is the trade-off between privacy and prediction accuracy?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Incorporating actual landing page text (rather than just URL character embeddings) into the information deficit computation will further improve word retention prediction F1 by at least 3 percentage points.

all_findings.finding: JointMap jointly learns commercial vs. non-commercial intent classification and product category mapping in e-commerce search via shared label-word attention and multi-task learning, achieving 2.3% Macro-F1 improvement on intent prediction and 10.9% on product category mapping over state-of-the-art single-task models.
all_findings.implication: Joint learning of commercial intent and product taxonomy mapping transfers inductive bias between tasks, improving both — especially benefiting minority product categories (8 minority classes improved from ~20% to 31.28% Macro-F1). This suggests that modeling hierarchical intent structures can overcome data sparsity in long-tail categories.
all_findings.open_question: Can the joint-learning approach extend to other e-commerce intent hierarchies (e.g., purchase intent + brand preference + price sensitivity)? How does the optimal task weighting vary with data volume per task?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A joint model for commercial intent + product category mapping will outperform separately trained models on both tasks when evaluated on held-out queries from an e-commerce search engine, with the largest gains on minority product categories.

all_findings.finding: Only 1.5% of e-commerce search queries (33M out of billions) have non-commercial intent (e.g., installation guides, store hours, discounts), yet they are critical for customer experience. The paper proposes distant supervision + active learning (SVM with confidence scoring + human re-labeling of tricky samples) to generate training data for this imbalanced class.
all_findings.implication: The extreme class imbalance (98.5% commercial vs. 1.5% non-commercial) means that standard supervised learning would ignore non-commercial intents entirely. The active learning pipeline reduces annotation cost while maintaining high inter-annotator agreement (Kappa=0.96).
all_findings.open_question: What is the distribution of non-commercial intents across different e-commerce domains (home improvement vs. apparel vs. electronics)? Can the distant supervision approach transfer across domains without retraining?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: An active learning pipeline that selects the most uncertain non-commercial queries for human labeling will achieve the same classification F1 as fully supervised training with 80% fewer labeled samples.

all_findings.finding: Focal loss (γ=1.5, borrowed from computer vision) applied to product category mapping improves Macro-F1 on 8 minority classes from 31.28% to 33.81% (8.1% relative improvement), though it slightly decreases overall Micro-F1 by 0.6% and Macro-F1 by 1.5%. Label representation learning (word-label attention) combined with multi-head self-attention forms the core of the model.
all_findings.implication: Focal loss is effective for extreme class imbalance in multi-label text classification, but requires careful γ tuning (optimal 1.5 for text vs. 2 for vision). The trade-off between overall accuracy and minority-class recall must be managed per application.
all_findings.open_question: How does focal loss compare with other imbalance-handling techniques (oversampling, cost-sensitive learning, synthetic data) for multi-label product categorization? Is the γ=1.5 finding stable across different taxonomies and e-commerce domains?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Focal loss with γ=1.5 will improve minority-class F1 by at least 5% relative to standard cross-entropy for multi-label product category classification across three different e-commerce taxonomies (home improvement, electronics, apparel).

all_findings.source: 2012.03_Wu_Brynjolfsson_The Future of Prediction_299.pdf
all_findings.finding: Google search volume for the category 'Real Estate Agencies' is strongly predictive of future housing sales: a 1 percentage point increase in the search index is associated with 67,220 additional quarterly housing sales per US state. Out-of-sample predictions using search data achieve MAE=0.102 vs. 0.441 for the baseline AR model without search data — a 75% improvement.
all_findings.implication: Aggregated search query data provides a real-time, fine-grained signal for economic forecasting that significantly outperforms traditional government statistics. This demonstrates that pre-purchase online information-seeking behavior is a leading indicator of actual economic transactions.
all_findings.open_question: How far ahead can search data reliably predict? Does the predictive power decay over longer horizons? Are there seasonal or event-driven perturbations that break the search-to-sales relationship?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A model using Google Trends search indices for 'Real Estate Agencies' will predict next-quarter US state-level housing sales with MAE at least 40% lower than a baseline model using only lagged housing statistics.

all_findings.source: 2012.03_Wu_Brynjolfsson_The Future of Prediction_299.pdf
all_findings.finding: Search data can distinguish supply from demand by refining categories: searches for 'home financing' correlate with housing price index (demand-side), while searches for 'home staging' correlate with housing supply. Past housing sales (lagged 2 quarters) predict later increases in home-appliance searches (β=1.14, p<0.01), showing ripple effects to complementary markets.
all_findings.implication: Fine-grained categorization of search queries enables decomposition of market dynamics into supply and demand components, which is typically impossible with aggregate economic statistics. This creates a new class of 'nanoeconomic' indicators for downstream economic activity.
all_findings.open_question: Can search-query-based supply/demand decomposition be validated against independent economic measures? How many query categories are needed for reliable decomposition across different markets?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Categorized search query indices (financing vs. staging vs. listings) will separately correlate with housing demand and supply measures (HPI vs. inventory) in a structural vector autoregression, outperforming undifferentiated 'real estate' search volume.

all_findings.source: 2012.03_Wu_Brynjolfsson_The Future of Prediction_299.pdf
all_findings.finding: The study uses state-level fixed-effects regression on 51 US states (DC included) over 2004–2009, with Google Trends query share (0–100 normalized index) rather than absolute volume. The search index for the broad 'Real Estate' category is not significantly correlated with sales — only the more specific 'Real Estate Agencies' subcategory shows strong predictive power.
all_findings.implication: The specificity of the search category is critical: broad categories dilute predictive signal with irrelevant sub-intents (property management, insurance). Researchers must carefully select or create query categories that directly map to the target economic behavior.
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal strategy for selecting query categories — data-driven discovery via topic modeling vs. expert-curated lists? How transferable are category definitions across time and regions?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Specific subcategory search indices (e.g., 'Real Estate Agencies') will consistently outperform broad category indices ('Real Estate') for predicting downstream economic activity across three different markets (housing, automotive, travel).

all_findings.source: 2023.findings-acl.474.pdf
all_findings.finding: TRENC (Tree-Transformer Encoders for Node Classification) achieves 80.93% macro-averaged F1 on zero-shot product type extraction from hub Web pages, outperforming the strongest baseline (GCN at 78.56%) by 2.37 absolute F1 points. The model encodes DOM trees with path attention (ancestor-descendant) and sibling attention mechanisms to model inter-node structural dependencies.
all_findings.implication: Specialized tree-structure-aware Transformers outperform generic GNNs for HTML DOM node classification, especially on deep trees. Structural attention mechanisms that mirror DOM hierarchy (path) and visual grouping (sibling) are critical for extracting structured information from semi-structured web content.
all_findings.open_question: How does TRENC scale to much larger DOM trees (e.g., 10K+ nodes)? Can the zero-shot SI generalization extend to abstract or non-physical shopping interests (e.g., 'productivity tools', 'home security')?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: TRENC will outperform GCN and GAT on product type extraction from DOM trees across all depth levels, with the performance gap widening (from 1% to >5%) for trees deeper than 7 levels.

all_findings.source: 2023.findings-acl.474.pdf
all_findings.finding: Text sequence is the most critical feature for PT extraction (removing it drops F1 by 22.95 points to 57.98). HTML tags (+2.11 F1) and shopping interest injection (+1.10 F1) provide smaller but consistent gains. Sibling attention ablation hurts more (-2.89 F1) than path attention ablation (-1.95 F1).
all_findings.implication: While structural information from DOM trees is valuable, the textual content of nodes dominates performance for this task. The larger drop from sibling attention than path attention suggests that sibling/peer context (e.g., list items) is more informative than hierarchical position for identifying product types.
all_findings.open_question: Can a model that jointly pretrains on both node text and DOM structure (via a masked-node prediction objective) outperform models that use frozen BERT embeddings? Would a larger PLM (LLaMA, GPT) as the text encoder improve zero-shot generalization?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A variant of TRENC that fine-tunes the PLM encoder jointly with the structural attention layers will outperform the fixed-embedding version by at least 3 F1 points on zero-shot PT extraction.

all_findings.source: 2023.findings-acl.474.pdf
all_findings.finding: The WEBPT dataset contains 453 hub pages covering 95 shopping interests with 94,167 DOM nodes (12,548 positive PT nodes). The zero-shot evaluation setup (no SI overlap between train/test splits) is challenging: cosine-similarity baselines achieve only 37.09 F1, and even heuristic rules outperform MLP (58.77 vs. 67.17 F1).
all_findings.implication: Zero-shot generalization across shopping interests is a hard problem — simple semantic similarity between SI names and product types fails because they are not semantically similar ('hiking' vs. 'headlamp'). The WEBPT benchmark provides a realistic evaluation for systems that must generalize to novel interests unseen during training.
all_findings.open_question: What is the upper bound of zero-shot PT extraction performance with a human oracle? Can few-shot fine-tuning with 1–5 examples per new SI approach full-supervised performance?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A few-shot PT extraction system fine-tuned on 5 labeled hub pages per new SI will achieve F1 within 5% of a fully supervised model trained on 50+ pages per SI.

all_findings.source: 2023.findings-acl.474.pdf
all_findings.finding: Common failure modes include: false positives on broad PT categories (e.g., 'Esthetics or Skin Care'), specific brand+product combos (e.g., 'Merrell Overlook Tall 2 WP Boot'), and non-purchasable items; false negatives occur when long descriptive text overwhelms the PT signal (e.g., 'Rods for River Fishing' as FN, 'Water bottle - 1 litre is good' as FN).
all_findings.implication: The model struggles at boundary cases that even humans find ambiguous (broad categories vs. specific products vs. non-purchasables). Long descriptions dilute the PT signal, suggesting that text length normalization or attention weighting by node depth could help.
all_findings.open_question: How should 'product type' be formally defined to reduce annotation ambiguity? Is a hierarchical taxonomy (broad category > specific type > branded product) needed for consistent evaluation?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: TRENC false positive rate on long descriptive nodes (>20 tokens) is at least 2× higher than on short nodes (<5 tokens), and can be reduced by incorporating a text-length-based confidence calibration.

all_findings.finding: GPT-based conjoint analysis produces WTP estimates that are sometimes comparable to human studies (within 50% for fluoride and aluminum in pilot studies) but often inaccurate or wrong-signed: GPT overestimates WTP for fluoride by ~3×, assigns wrong sign to new toothpaste flavors (cucumber, pancake), shows negative WTP for larger laptop screens, and substantially misestimates brand preferences for Macbooks and iPads.
all_findings.implication: LLMs cannot be relied upon as drop-in replacements for human surveys in market research. Without a human benchmark, researchers have no basis to identify which estimates are unreliable. The approach works best when combined with human data, not as a substitute.
all_findings.open_question: Can newer LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5) significantly reduce the error rates observed with GPT-3.5 Turbo? Is there a systematic pattern to the failures (e.g., overvaluing novel features, undervaluing established brands)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: LLM responses to conjoint surveys will systematically overestimate WTP for novel/hypothetical product features (e.g., built-in projector) by at least 2× compared to human benchmarks, while underestimating brand equity for established brands.

all_findings.finding: Fine-tuning GPT with human survey data from the same product category and population substantially improves alignment, correcting wrong signs on new flavors and bringing WTP estimates for new features close to human benchmarks. However, fine-tuning does not improve (and sometimes worsens) performance when the target involves a different product category or different demographic segment.
all_findings.implication: Fine-tuning transfers category-specific preference knowledge but not generalizable preference understanding. The most defensible use case is within-category extrapolation: estimate WTP for new features within a product category where human survey data already exists. Cross-category and cross-demographic generalization requires additional human data.
all_findings.open_question: What volume of fine-tuning data is sufficient for reliable within-category extrapolation? Can multi-category fine-tuning or multi-task learning enable cross-category transfer?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A GPT model fine-tuned on human conjoint data from 3 product categories (toothpaste, laptops, tablets) will show improved WTP alignment for a 4th held-out category compared to zero-shot GPT, but will still underperform category-specific fine-tuning.

all_findings.finding: GPT fails to reflect meaningful preference heterogeneity across demographic groups (gender, income, political affiliation) despite multiple prompting strategies and fine-tuning approaches. Even with fine-tuning, the model reflects average population WTP but does not differentiate across segments.
all_findings.implication: Current LLMs are not suitable for segment-level market research or targeted marketing analytics that require understanding subgroup preferences. Researchers needing demographic-specific insights must still conduct human studies for each segment.
all_findings.open_question: Would larger models (GPT-4, Claude 3) with demographic-conditioned training capture heterogeneity better? Is the failure due to training data composition (underrepresentation of minority demographics) or fundamental model architecture limitations?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: LLM-generated WTP estimates conditioned on demographic attributes (income, gender, age) will show less variance across demographic groups than human survey data for the same attributes, with F-test confirming significantly narrower between-group differences.

all_findings.finding: The study demonstrates that LLMs can serve as a supplement to—not a substitute for—human studies. The most promising use case is within-category extrapolation where prior human survey data exists for the relevant product category and population, allowing fine-tuned LLMs to generate preliminary estimates for new features before investing in a full human study.
all_findings.implication: The recommended workflow is: conduct human conjoint studies for a product category → fine-tune LLM → generate preliminary WTP estimates for new features → validate with a smaller targeted human study. This hybrid approach reduces the cost and time of market research while maintaining reliability.
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal ratio of human-to-LLM data in a hybrid market research pipeline? How should confidence intervals be computed for LLM-based WTP estimates?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A hybrid market research pipeline (human study on 150 participants + fine-tuned LLM on 10 features + targeted validation on 50 participants) will produce WTP estimates within 20% of a full-scale human study (300 participants) at 40% of the cost.

all_findings.finding: Painsight, an unsupervised framework using gradient-based attribution on BERT sentiment and topic models, extracts pain points from customer reviews without ground-truth labels. Human evaluation shows Painsight outperforms baselines: Task 1 (pain point candidate extraction) scores 3.60/5 vs. spaCy 2.95/5; Task 2 (category-level pain points) scores 0.72 vs. spaCy 0.55; Task 3 (topic-aware pain points) scores 0.47 vs. BERTopic 0.33.
all_findings.implication: Gradient-based attribution on weakly-supervised tasks (sentiment analysis, topic modeling) can effectively localize pain point keywords without supervised annotation. The ITM (Iterative Topic Modification) algorithm reduces BERTopic outliers from ~57% to ~32%, enabling more comprehensive topic coverage.
all_findings.open_question: How does Painsight generalize to non-Korean languages and non-home-appliance product categories? Does gradient-based attribution work equally well for longer reviews where pain points span multiple sentences?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Painsight will extract pain points from English product reviews with comparable human-evaluation scores (within 10% of Korean results) when using a multilingual BERT backbone, across 5 diverse product categories.

all_findings.finding: The definition of pain points requires distinguishing general negative sentiment from specific, actionable complaints. 'Not good' is not a pain point, but 'battery drains too fast' is. The framework filters reviews using a keyword dictionary and stopwords, then uses attribution scores from sentiment (negative class) and topic classifiers to identify tokens most influential to predictions.
all_findings.implication: Pain points are a distinct construct from sentiment or aspect categories — they require specific, addressable references to product deficiencies. The operational definition (high-attribution tokens in negative reviews, refined by dependency parsing for NP/VP structure) enables automated extraction without domain-specific annotation.
all_findings.open_question: Can pain points be automatically categorized into types (usability, performance, reliability, feature gap) based on linguistic patterns in the extracted tokens? Is there a universal taxonomy of pain points across product categories?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Pain point tokens extracted via Painsight will cluster into 4-6 universal categories (performance, usability, reliability, compatibility, cost, support) across 10+ product categories using unsupervised topic modeling on the extracted pain point phrases.

all_findings.finding: The dataset comprises 490K+ Korean customer reviews across 5 home appliance categories (dryer, styler, washing machine, vacuum cleaner, robotic vacuum) from 17 sources. BERT + vocabulary expansion achieves 98.23% accuracy on sentiment analysis. ITM outperforms BERTopic on NPMI coherence in 3/5 categories while reducing outlier percentage from 57% to 32% on average.
all_findings.implication: Domain-specific vocabulary expansion provides measurable gains for BERT on review text, suggesting that pre-trained models benefit from incorporating domain terminology. The high sentiment accuracy (>98%) indicates that review sentiment is relatively easy to classify for home appliances, but pain point extraction remains challenging.
all_findings.open_question: Would Painsight benefit from multilingual pre-training or cross-lingual transfer for pain point extraction? Does the 98%+ sentiment accuracy plateau leave room for attribution quality improvement through better (not just more accurate) models?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Using a sentiment model with >99% accuracy (ensemble or larger PLM) will not significantly improve Painsight's pain point extraction quality, as attribution quality depends more on model internals than prediction accuracy beyond a threshold.

all_findings.finding: ARALLM enables non-expert marketers to input natural language targeting demands and receive structured logical expressions (SELL) for user targeting. The analogical reasoning prompting approach retrieves similar examples from a reasoning library to guide LLM reasoning, achieving 58.5 S-BLEU vs. 37.0 for zero-shot and 42.0 for fixed few-shot on GPT-3.5.
all_findings.implication: Retrieving similar reasoning examples (analogical reasoning) is significantly more effective than fixed few-shot examples for structured language translation tasks. The reasoning library of 100 high-quality examples with reasoning steps provides sufficient coverage for diverse marketer demands.
all_findings.open_question: How does the analogical reasoning approach scale to different structured output formats (JSON, SQL, HTML)? Is the 100-example library size generalizable or task-specific?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Analogical reasoning prompting (retrieving similar examples) will outperform fixed few-shot prompting by at least 15% S-BLEU on NL2SQL and NL2JSON tasks, consistent with the NL2SELL results.

all_findings.finding: Knowledge distillation from GPT-3.5 to smaller models (Baichuan2-13B-Chat, ChatGLM2-6B-32K) with multi-task training (answer prediction + reasoning step prediction) achieves better NL2SELL performance than the teacher: Baichuan2-13B-Chat reaches 62.2 S-BLEU vs. GPT-3.5's 59.7. The answer-to-demand distillation (generating synthetic demands from random SELL templates) adds robustness, improving performance from 58.9 to 62.2 S-BLEU.
all_findings.implication: Smaller fine-tuned models can outperform their LLM teacher when trained with multi-task objectives and synthetic data augmentation. The answer-to-demand pipeline addresses the long-tail distribution problem by generating training data for rare tag combinations that are underrepresented in natural marketer demands.
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal student model size for deployment vs. performance trade-off? Can the multi-task distillation approach extend to other NL-to-structured-language tasks?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A multi-task distilled 7B model trained with reasoning-augmented objectives will consistently outperform a same-size model trained only on answer pairs across 3 different NL-to-structured-language benchmarks.

all_findings.finding: Real-world deployment on Ant Group's Alipay platform shows ARALLM reduces average marketer operation time from 12-40 minutes to ~3 minutes (4-10× faster), receives 1.3× more likes from marketers, and produces significant CTR improvements in A/B testing: digital car campaign +55.21%, housing provident fund +30.56%, ophthalmic health +69.17%, TCM +112.7%, recharge +73.89%.
all_findings.implication: Structured understanding of natural language demands has substantial practical impact in industrial marketing systems. The CTR improvements (30-113%) suggest that better targeting (enabled by more expressive and accurate audience definitions) directly translates to business value.
all_findings.open_question: Which NL2SELL components contributed most to the CTR lift — improved tag selection, better logical structure, or reduced marketer error? Can the approach generalize beyond Ant Group's ecosystem to other marketing platforms?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Automated NL-to-SELL conversion will consistently improve campaign CTR by at least 20% compared to manual rule-based targeting (marketers selecting tags through dropdown menus) across a diverse set of digital marketing campaigns.

all_findings.finding: The study identifies a critical gap in user targeting systems: marketers must manually decompose natural language demands into tags/entities, which is unfriendly, time-consuming, and error-prone. The SELL language (Structured and Editable Logical Language) defines keys, values, operators (Equal To, Greater Than, Belongs To, etc.), and AND/OR combinators to represent targeting conditions clearly.
all_findings.implication: SELL fills a gap between natural language and database query languages: it is simpler than SQL (no JOINs, no nested queries) but more expressive than tag-based selection (supports range comparisons, boolean logic). This intermediate representation is both human-readable and machine-executable.
all_findings.open_question: Can SELL be extended to temporal conditions (e.g., 'users who bought X in the last 30 days') and behavioral sequences without losing simplicity? Is SELL generalizable beyond marketing to other domain-specific targeting (hiring, education, healthcare)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Non-technical users (marketers, product managers) will be able to read and manually edit SELL expressions with >90% accuracy after 10 minutes of training, compared to <50% accuracy for SQL equivalents.

all_findings.finding: LASER applies retrieval-based speculative decoding to LLM-based recommender systems, achieving 3-5× speedup on public datasets (MovieLens-10M, Amazon-Books) and saving ~67% of computational resources in an online A/B test on a large-scale advertising scenario, with lossless downstream recommendation performance (AUC and Logloss virtually unchanged).
all_findings.implication: Speculative decoding is highly effective for LLM-based knowledge generation in RSs because old knowledge naturally serves as a retrieval pool for new knowledge generation. This enables practical deployment of LLMs for industrial-scale recommendation scenarios where generating knowledge for millions of users would otherwise be prohibitively expensive (e.g., 565 GPU days for 10M users with Qwen-7B).
all_findings.open_question: Can speculative decoding be applied to other LLM-based RS tasks beyond knowledge generation (e.g., direct item generation, explanation generation)? How does the speedup degrade as the ratio of new-to-old users/items increases?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: LASER will maintain >2× speedup even when 50% of users are new (no old knowledge in retrieval pool) by leveraging attribute-based retrieval pools for cold-start users, though the speedup will be lower than for existing users.

all_findings.finding: Two key RS properties enable further acceleration: (1) recommendation tasks exhibit high diversity tolerance — different LLM-generated knowledge texts (from top-k sampling) produce nearly identical downstream AUC (differences <0.1%); (2) retrieval inefficiency grows with pool size — a global pool for all users leads to retrieval time >20% of total generation time, negating acceleration benefits.
all_findings.implication: Standard speculative decoding's greedy verification (accepting only the highest-probability token) is unnecessarily strict for RS knowledge generation. Relaxing verification to top-k tokens with a probability threshold increases acceptance rate without harming downstream quality. Customized retrieval pools (collaborative-based for existing users, attribute-based for new items) keep pools small and relevant.
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal trade-off between retrieval pool size (speed) and coverage (acceptance rate)? Can the binary router be learned (instead of rule-based) for assigning users/items to pool types?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Customized retrieval pools (grouped by collaborative clustering or attributes) will achieve higher generation speed than a single global pool for any pool size >100 entries, due to lower retrieval time and comparable acceptance rates.

all_findings.finding: LASER's relaxed verification (top-2 tokens with probability threshold p=0.1) increases average acceptance length (AAL) from 5.51 to 7.54 on ML-10M while maintaining similar total generated tokens (no divergent generation). Using top-k without the probability threshold causes divergent generation (30-50% more tokens). The customized retrieval pool reduces average retrieval time from 1.019s (global) to 0.064s on ML-10M.
all_findings.implication: The combination of custom retrieval pools and relaxed verification is synergistic: smaller pools reduce retrieval time while relaxed verification increases tokens accepted per step. The probability threshold is critical to prevent divergent generation that would negate speedup gains.
all_findings.open_question: Can the optimal k and p values be dynamically adjusted per generation based on observed acceptance rates? Would a learned acceptance criterion outperform the fixed top-k + threshold approach?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: LASER's relaxed verification (top-k + probability threshold) will produce generated texts with higher semantic similarity to autoregressive texts than greedy verification applied to sampled (non-retrieved) drafts, despite accepting fewer identical tokens.

all_findings.finding: LASER consistently outperforms other speculative decoding methods (Lookahead, SpecInfer, EAGLE, REST) across 4 conditions (ML-item, ML-user, AMZ-item, AMZ-user), achieving 4-5× speedup vs. REST (1.5-2.5×), EAGLE (1.5-2.5×), SpecInfer (1-2×), and Lookahead (1-1.5×). It also works across diverse backbone LLMs (Mistral-7B, ChatGLM2-6B, Vicuna-7B, Qwen-7B/1.8B).
all_findings.implication: Retrieval-based speculative decoding is particularly well-suited for recommendation knowledge generation because of the natural overlap between old and new knowledge. LASER's RS-specific optimizations (customized pools, relaxed verification) provide 2-3× additional speedup over generic retrieval-based methods like REST.
all_findings.open_question: Does the efficiency advantage of retrieval-based methods over model-based drafters (EAGLE, SpecInfer) generalize to other domains with natural retrieval pools (e.g., code generation from existing codebases, document summarization from related documents)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Retrieval-based speculative decoding will outperform model-based drafting (EAGLE, SpecInfer) for code completion tasks where existing code in the repository serves as the retrieval pool, achieving at least 2× higher speedup.

all_findings.source: Representing Tasks with a Graph-Based Method for Supporting Users in Complex Search Tasks.pdf
all_findings.finding: TEXT DUMP QUALITY: This text dump contains only page break markers with no extractable text content. The PDF was likely scanned or image-based and could not be reliably converted to text. No substantive findings can be extracted.
all_findings.implication: This source cannot be processed further without alternative extraction methods (OCR, manual transcription). Document should be flagged for re-processing with an OCR pipeline.
all_findings.open_question: Would OCR-based extraction (Tesseract, Azure OCR) recover meaningful content from this document? What proportion of the web-sources collection requires OCR?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: OCR processing of this PDF will extract at least 500 words of usable text, enabling identification of at least 2 research concepts relevant to graph-based task representation.

all_findings.source: Revealed_Attention.pdf (Masatlioglu, Nakajima & Ozbay, 2012 AER)
all_findings.finding: Formal model of 'choice with limited attention' (CLA) where decision makers maximize a stable preference within an 'attention filter' — a consideration set that does not change when an unattended alternative becomes unavailable. The model is characterized by a weakening of WARP called WARP(LA): for any set S, there exists x* such that for any T including x*, if c(T) ∈ S and c(T) ≠ c(T\x*), then c(T) = x*.
all_findings.implication: The attention filter axiom (Γ(S) = Γ(S\x) whenever x ∉ Γ(S)) provides a minimal, normatively appealing structure for modeling limited consideration without specifying how consideration sets are formed. This enables revealed preference analysis without the full attention assumption: choice reversals when removing an unchosen alternative simultaneously reveal attention (the removed alternative was noticed) and preference (the chosen alternative is preferred to the removed one).
all_findings.open_question: Can the attention filter model be empirically distinguished from other models of limited consideration (rationalization, sequential elimination) using field data rather than lab experiments? What kinds of choice data are needed?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Consumer choice data from online shopping environments (e.g., Amazon browse-to-purchase paths) will exhibit WARP(LA) violations more frequently than WARP violations, consistent with limited attention, and the pattern of violations will match the attention filter model better than rationalization or sequential elimination alternatives.

all_findings.source: Revealed_Attention.pdf (Masatlioglu, Nakajima & Ozbay, 2012 AER)
all_findings.finding: Theorem 1 (Revealed Preference): x is revealed preferred to y iff x P_R y, where P is defined as x P y if there exists T such that c(T) = x ≠ c(T\y), and P_R is its transitive closure. Theorem 2 (Revealed Attention/Inattention): x is revealed not to attract attention at S iff x P_R c(S); x is revealed to attract attention at S iff there exists T where removing x causes choice reversal. Welfare analysis is possible even with non-standard choices.
all_findings.implication: The revealed preference and attention results provide practical tools for analyzing choice data: a policymaker can identify which products consumers truly prefer vs. which are merely overlooked. This has direct application to competition policy (is a product unpopular because consumers dislike it or because they never consider it?), online search ranking, and retail shelf placement.
all_findings.open_question: How many choice observations are needed to reliably estimate revealed preferences under limited attention? Does the transitive closure P_R converge quickly or require large datasets?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Using simulated choice data from the CLA model, the transitive closure P_R will correctly identify >90% of true preference relations with as few as 50 choice observations per consumer across 15-20 menus, demonstrating practical identifiability.

all_findings.source: Revealed_Attention.pdf (Masatlioglu, Nakajima & Ozbay, 2012 AER)
all_findings.finding: The model accommodates frequently observed anomalies that standard theory cannot: Attraction Effect (adding an irrelevant decoy changes choice), Cyclical Choice (x chosen over y, y over z, z over x — driven by attention patterns, not preference reversals), and Choosing Pairwisely Unchosen (an alternative never chosen in pairwise comparisons is chosen from larger sets). These are explained solely by limited attention, without introducing changing preferences.
all_findings.implication: Many 'irrational' choice patterns in consumer behavior can be explained parsimoniously by limited attention rather than unstable preferences or context dependence. This has important implications for welfare analysis: the Bernheim-Rangel model-free approach would draw opposite welfare conclusions from the same data (e.g., declaring y welfare-superior to x when in fact x is preferred but overlooked).
all_findings.open_question: What fraction of observed violations of standard choice axioms in field data can be attributed to limited attention vs. genuine preference instability? Can the CLA model be used as a diagnostic tool?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: In a re-analysis of 3 published datasets showing WARP violations (e.g., from behavioral economics experiments), at least 40% of violations will be consistent with the CLA model (satisfying WARP(LA)) and thus explainable by limited attention rather than preference change.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: Customer needs are abstract context-dependent statements describing benefits customers seek, distinct from product attributes.
all_findings.implication: Search-intelligence must distinguish between surface-level query patterns and underlying user intents/JTBD.
all_findings.open_question: How do we map search queries to abstract JTBD statements vs. product attributes?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Sentence-level intent labels outperform keyword-cluster labels for downstream product development decisions.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: Traditional VOC uses 20-30 qualitative experiential interviews producing ~100 abstract customer-need statements structured hierarchically.
all_findings.implication: Ground-truth target: ~100 intent clusters per domain, structured hierarchically (3 levels).
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal granularity (number of clusters) for search intent taxonomies?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A 3-level hierarchy provides the best balance of interpretability and coverage.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: 52% of UGC sentences contained at least one customer need; 9.2% contained two or more needs. 10% of most frequent needs appear in 54% of informative sentences.
all_findings.implication: Search queries follow power-law distribution: few dominant intents, many rare ones.
all_findings.open_question: How many rare intents must a search-intelligence product capture to provide value?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Capturing the top 10% of intent clusters covers >50% of search volume.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: Inter-coder accuracy was 70% (new analyst vs. three original analysts). Individual analysts identified 45-68% of universe needs.
all_findings.implication: Human agreement on intent labeling is inherently ~70-80%. Use multiple coders.
all_findings.open_question: What is acceptable inter-annotator agreement for search-intent taxonomies?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Majority-vote from 3 annotators achieves 80%+ precision on search intent labels.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: CNN achieves F1=74.0% for identifying informative sentences. LSTM-RNN: 73.4%. SVM: 65.7%. CNN with asymmetric costs: recall 85.3%, precision 65.2%.
all_findings.implication: Deep learning classifiers outperform SVM by ~8-10 F1 points for informativeness detection.
all_findings.open_question: What is the best architecture for identifying intent-bearing search queries?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A BERT-based classifier achieves >80% F1 for informative query detection.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: CNN performance stabilizes after ~500 training sentences, with improvement up to 2,000+. Training under 1 minute on MacBook Pro for 1,000-4,000 samples.
all_findings.implication: Labeling budgets can be modest: ~500-2,000 labeled examples.
all_findings.open_question: What is the minimum labeled sample size for robust query informativeness classification?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: 500 labeled queries achieve >90% of the F1 achievable with 5,000 labels.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: CNN identified sentences containing 100% of frequent needs, 91% of rare needs, and 84% of new-to-holdout needs.
all_findings.implication: Informativeness filters have different recall by frequency class. Frequency-stratified evaluation required.
all_findings.open_question: Do intent classifiers show systematic bias against rare intents?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Intent classifiers achieve 90%+ recall on high-frequency intents but <70% on long-tail intents.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: Sentence embeddings (20-dim from word embedding averages) clustered with Ward's method separate primary needs in PCA.
all_findings.implication: Simple averaged word embeddings + hierarchical clustering can separate high-level intent categories.
all_findings.open_question: What embedding granularity best separates fine-grained search intents?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: BERT sentence embeddings achieve higher cluster purity than averaged Word2Vec by >15%.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: ML reduces manual review effort by 15-22%. At 200 sentences: 78% efficiency; at 400: 79%.
all_findings.implication: ML pre-filtering reduces human labeling effort by 15-22%.
all_findings.open_question: What is the cost savings of ML-assisted vs. fully manual taxonomy building?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: ML-filtered sampling identifies 20%+ more unique intents per dollar spent on annotation.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: Frequency of mention is marginally correlated with importance (ρ=0.21, p=0.06). Using only above-median frequency would miss 29% of high-importance needs.
all_findings.implication: Frequency-based or popularity-based query clustering misses rare but strategically valuable intents.
all_findings.open_question: How to distinguish rare-but-important intents from noise?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Embedding-based similarity to known high-value intents identifies rare important queries.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: Oral-care VOC hierarchy: 6 primary groups, 22 secondary groups, 86 tertiary needs.
all_findings.implication: Search-intent taxonomy prototype can target 5-7 primary, 15-25 secondary categories.
all_findings.open_question: What is a universal intent taxonomy schema that generalizes across categories?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A 6-primary/22-secondary taxonomy fits most physical product categories.

all_findings.source: Timoshenko_Hauser_Customer_Needs_from_UGC.pdf
all_findings.finding: Word embeddings trained with skip-gram, k=5 window, d=20 dimensions. Sentence embeddings: average of word embeddings.
all_findings.implication: Simple averaging of word embeddings is a strong, scalable baseline for intent clustering.
all_findings.open_question: Does mean-pooling of BERT token embeddings outperform learned sentence embeddings for intent clustering?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Sentence-BERT embeddings improve NMI for intent clusters by 10+ points over averaged word embeddings.

all_findings.source: jansen_patterns_query_reformulation.pdf
all_findings.finding: 6 query-reformulation states: New, Assistance, Content Change, Generalization, Reformulation, Specialization. Classified 964,780 sessions.
all_findings.implication: Search behavior has 6 distinct reformulation types. Intent tracking should capture reformulation patterns.
all_findings.open_question: Do query-reformulation states correspond to distinct user intents/JTBD?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Reformulation states correlate with shifts in JTBD categories.

all_findings.source: jansen_patterns_query_reformulation.pdf
all_findings.finding: 71.64% of sessions contain only 1 query; 15.85% have 2 queries; 12.51% have 3+. Mean query length: 2.79 terms.
all_findings.implication: Most sessions are single-query. Short queries dominate. Intent extraction must work with very short text.
all_findings.open_question: How to extract reliable intents from single short queries (2-3 words)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Query expansion via retrieved documents improves single-query intent classification by 20%+.

all_findings.source: jansen_patterns_query_reformulation.pdf
all_findings.finding: Transition probability: from New state, 21% go to Reformulation, 22% to Specialization, 21% to Assistance. After Assistance, 58% go to Content Change.
all_findings.implication: Users often reformulate or specialize immediately after initial query.
all_findings.open_question: Can query-reformulation patterns predict intent shifts within a session?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A sequence model over reformulation states predicts intent shifts with >70% accuracy.

all_findings.source: jansen_patterns_query_reformulation.pdf
all_findings.finding: Misclassification analysis: 95.6% accuracy on 2,000 verified queries. Errors: misspelling (58.5%), cookie issues (25.8%), special characters (5.6%).
all_findings.implication: Automated query classification feasible at >95% accuracy. Main errors: spelling normalization.
all_findings.open_question: How robust are intent classifiers to query spelling variations?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Spell-normalization preprocessor improves intent classification accuracy by 3-5%.

all_findings.source: jansen_patterns_query_reformulation.pdf
all_findings.finding: Second-order Markov model: precision 40%, coverage 13%. Best overall tradeoff.
all_findings.implication: 2nd-order Markov best for reformulation prediction. Only last 2 states needed.
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal session context window for intent prediction?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A transformer with context window of 3-5 queries outperforms 2nd-order Markov.

all_findings.source: IR_Survey_BeiWang_2026.pdf
all_findings.finding: Two core IR tasks: extracting features, developing relevance estimators. Evolution: BM25 → dense retrieval (BERT) → LLM-based retrieval.
all_findings.implication: Intent clustering needs representation backbone + relevance/clustering estimator. Separable decisions.
all_findings.open_question: Should representation and clustering share a model or be separate?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Separate representation and clustering models outperform end-to-end models.

all_findings.source: IR_Survey_BeiWang_2026.pdf
all_findings.finding: Modern IR follows 'retrieve-then-rerank' two-stage: bi-encoder selects top-k, cross-encoder refines.
all_findings.implication: Two-stage clustering: fast bi-encoder clustering + cross-encoder refinement.
all_findings.open_question: Is two-stage clustering more effective than single-stage?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Two-stage pipeline improves cluster quality by 15% over single-stage.

all_findings.source: IR_Survey_BeiWang_2026.pdf
all_findings.finding: Hard Negative Mining critical: In-Batch Negatives, Lexical Negatives (BM25), Iterative HNM (ANCE).
all_findings.implication: Careful negative sampling improves embedding quality for distinguishing similar intents.
all_findings.open_question: What negative sampling strategy best learns separable intent embeddings?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Combined IBN + lexical negatives produces 10% better intent separation than random negatives.

all_findings.source: IR_Survey_BeiWang_2026.pdf
all_findings.finding: Knowledge Distillation: student bi-encoder trained to mimic cross-encoder via KL divergence. Bridges effectiveness gap.
all_findings.implication: Distilled bi-encoder can approach cross-encoder quality at fraction of cost.
all_findings.open_question: Can distilled bi-encoder match cross-encoder quality for intent assignment?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Distilled bi-encoder achieves <5% quality gap with cross-encoder while 100x faster.

all_findings.source: IR_Survey_BeiWang_2026.pdf
all_findings.finding: Sparse retrieval (SPLADE): learned sparse vectors aligned to vocabulary, inverted-index compatible, CPU inference.
all_findings.implication: Sparse intent representations offer interpretability and CPU-efficient deployment.
all_findings.open_question: Are sparse or dense representations better for intent interpretability?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Sparse representations achieve higher human-interpretability scores for cluster labeling.

all_findings.source: IR_Survey_BeiWang_2026.pdf
all_findings.finding: Multi-vector models (ColBERT): token-level late interaction via MaxSim. Strong performance with efficient index.
all_findings.implication: Multi-vector representations may capture multi-faceted intents better than single-vector.
all_findings.open_question: Do multi-vector representations improve handling of multi-faceted queries?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: ColBERT-style multi-vector improves recall for multi-intent queries by 15%.

all_findings.source: IR_Survey_BeiWang_2026.pdf
all_findings.finding: LLM as retriever: scaling law - larger backbone LMs (7B+) improve bi-encoder retrieval. LLM2Vec enables bidirectional attention on decoder-only models.
all_findings.implication: Larger embedding models outperform BERT-size. Decoder adaptation needed for Llama-family.
all_findings.open_question: What is cost-benefit sweet spot for embedding model size?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A 7B embedding model improves NMI by 10+ points over 350M BERT.

all_findings.source: IR_Survey_BeiWang_2026.pdf
all_findings.finding: LLM as reranker: pointwise (T5), pairwise, listwise (RankGPT). Zero-shot RankGPT-4 competitive without fine-tuning.
all_findings.implication: LLMs can assign queries to intent clusters zero-shot. Useful for seed taxonomy creation.
all_findings.open_question: Can prompted LLMs replace human annotation for intent taxonomy creation?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: GPT-4 zero-shot labeling achieves 80%+ agreement with expert annotators.

all_findings.source: IR_Survey_BeiWang_2026.pdf
all_findings.finding: Contrastive learning (InfoNCE) for bi-encoder training: maximizes similarity between query-positive document vs. negatives.
all_findings.implication: Contrastive training is the standard for learning search-intent representations.
all_findings.open_question: What constitutes a positive pair for intent representation learning?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Co-clicked documents as positive pairs produce better intent representations than same-session queries.

all_findings.source: EECS-2022-178.pdf
all_findings.finding: Four fundamental problems: Measurement (experimentation often infeasible), Feedback (recommendations affect future behavior), Scale, Incentives.
all_findings.implication: Search-intent system must account for feedback loops, measurement bias, and strategic manipulation.
all_findings.open_question: How do feedback loops bias intent clusters?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Intent clusters from pre-recommendation logs differ systematically from post-recommendation logs.

all_findings.source: EECS-2022-178.pdf
all_findings.finding: Offline metrics predict online performance across 6 simulated environments. Spearman rank correlations positive. But diminishing returns after a point.
all_findings.implication: Offline metrics valid for screening methods, but chasing small improvements may not yield online gains.
all_findings.open_question: What is the good enough threshold for offline cluster quality?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Beyond NMI=0.75, further improvements yield diminishing online gains.

all_findings.source: EECS-2022-178.pdf
all_findings.finding: Feedback loops affect algorithms differently. TopPop improves over time. CF-NADE improves slower. AutoRec initially bad but quickly matches best.
all_findings.implication: Some clustering algorithms more susceptible to feedback bias than others.
all_findings.open_question: Which intent clustering methods are most/least susceptible to feedback-loop bias?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Frequency-based grouping exhibits stronger feedback amplification than embedding-based clustering.

all_findings.source: EECS-2022-178.pdf
all_findings.finding: Higher embedding dimension (d=50 vs d=10) induces more bias in content targeting. More expressive algorithms incentivize more targeted content.
all_findings.implication: Higher-dimensional embeddings may enable more biased or manipulative targeting.
all_findings.open_question: What is the safe dimensionality that balances expressiveness and bias?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Intent embeddings with d<30 show 50% less demographic bias amplification than d>100.

all_findings.source: EECS-2022-178.pdf
all_findings.finding: Pre-deployment audit of strategic creator incentives can identify misalignment before deployment.
all_findings.implication: Simulate strategic responses before deploying an intent-clustering system.
all_findings.open_question: What is a pre-deployment audit framework for search-intent systems?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Pre-deployment simulation predicts 70%+ of real-world content manipulation patterns.

all_findings.source: Intent-Based Browse Activity Segmentation.pdf
all_findings.finding: User browse activity can be segmented into intent-related logical segments automatically using features from browsing behavior rather than relying on fixed inactivity timeouts
all_findings.implication: Users exhibit multiple distinct intents within a single browsing session; segmentation should be based on content/topic shifts, not just time gaps
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal granularity of intent segments for downstream tasks like ad targeting or personalization?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Intent-based segmentation of browsing logs improves ad relevance prediction compared to time-window-based segmentation

all_findings.source: Intent-Based Browse Activity Segmentation.pdf
all_findings.finding: Proposed method uses features like URL domain shifts, query reformulations, and inter-request time deltas to detect intent boundaries in user search/browse activity
all_findings.implication: Multiple feature types (temporal, topical, lexical) are needed to reliably detect intent boundaries; no single signal suffices
all_findings.open_question: Which combination of features provides the best segmentation accuracy across different user populations?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A model combining URL domain change, query topic shift, and pause duration features outperforms models using any single feature type for intent segmentation

all_findings.source: Intent-Based Browse Activity Segmentation.pdf
all_findings.finding: Supervised machine learning approach to browsing log segmentation outperforms heuristic timeout-based methods
all_findings.implication: A trained segmentation model can replace fixed 30-minute session cutoffs used in most analytics systems
all_findings.open_question: How well does a segmentation model trained on one search engine's logs transfer to another platform's browsing data?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A supervised segmentation model reduces within-segment topic variance by >30% compared to fixed 30-minute session boundaries

all_findings.source: Intent-Based Browse Activity Segmentation.pdf
all_findings.finding: Feature importance analysis shows that content-based features (URL, page title) contribute more to segmentation accuracy than temporal features
all_findings.implication: Future systems should prioritize content-based signals over time-based heuristics for intent detection
all_findings.open_question: How do content-based features perform on encrypted traffic where URL content is not available?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Content features (URL topic, page category) have 2x the predictive power of temporal features for intent boundary detection

all_findings.source: Query2Interest Classification at Pinterest.pdf
all_findings.finding: Short text (query) classification to predict user interests is challenging because queries have <5 words on average, making traditional NLP approaches less effective
all_findings.implication: Standard pre-trained models like BERT may not perform optimally on very short inputs without adaptation
all_findings.open_question: What is the minimum query length below which BERT-based models degrade significantly compared to simpler models?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: BERT-based models show <5% improvement over fastText on queries with <3 words, but show >10% improvement on queries with >5 words

all_findings.source: Query2Interest Classification at Pinterest.pdf
all_findings.finding: Novel pin annotation-based query augmentation algorithm significantly boosts classification precision across all model architectures tested
all_findings.implication: Weakly supervised data augmentation from user engagement signals (pins, saves, clicks) can dramatically improve short text classification without additional human labeling
all_findings.open_question: How does pin-annotation augmentation compare to other query augmentation methods like back-translation or paraphrasing?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Query augmentation using user engagement signals (pins) improves F1 by >8% for short query classification compared to training on raw queries alone

all_findings.source: Query2Interest Classification at Pinterest.pdf
all_findings.finding: With pin annotation augmentation, simpler models like fastText achieve performance competitive with complex DNNs (BERT, XLNet, GPT-2) while being orders of magnitude faster and cheaper to deploy
all_findings.implication: Data augmentation can close the gap between simple and complex models, making simpler models viable for production where latency and cost matter
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal augmentation strategy that maximizes simple model performance while minimizing computational cost?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: fastText with query augmentation achieves within 2% of BERT's accuracy on Q2I classification while being 100x faster at inference time

all_findings.source: Query2Interest Classification at Pinterest.pdf
all_findings.finding: Multi-modality data augmentation (using both text and image signals from pinned content) boosts classification precision and allows simpler models to work as well as complex DNNs
all_findings.implication: Leveraging multi-modal signals from user engagement can compensate for short text sparsity in classification tasks
all_findings.open_question: Which modality (text vs image) contributes more to the augmentation signal for query understanding?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Multi-modal augmentation (text+image) yields 3-5% higher accuracy than text-only augmentation for Q2I classification

all_findings.source: Query2Interest Classification at Pinterest.pdf
all_findings.finding: Pre-trained models fine-tuned on Q2I data show transfer learning benefits but the gains are modest compared to the computational cost; BERT achieves ~86% accuracy
all_findings.implication: For short-text classification, the marginal benefit of large pre-trained models may not justify their deployment cost when data augmentation bridges the gap
all_findings.open_question: Are there types of queries or interest categories where BERT significantly outperforms augmented fastText?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: BERT's advantage over augmented fastText on Q2I is limited to <3% accuracy points, concentrated on rare/ambiguous queries

all_findings.source: SimCSE- Simple Contrastive Learning of Sentence Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: Standard dropout alone acts as minimal data augmentation for contrastive learning of sentence embeddings; predicting a sentence from itself with different dropout masks is sufficient for unsupervised learning
all_findings.implication: Contrastive learning can work with trivial/noise augmentation rather than requiring heavy data augmentation - this is a breakthrough finding for sentence embedding quality
all_findings.open_question: Why does dropout serve as an effective augmentation for sentence-level contrastive learning but not for other domains?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Removing dropout from SimCSE causes complete representation collapse, while adding stronger augmentation does not further improve performance

all_findings.source: SimCSE- Simple Contrastive Learning of Sentence Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: Unsupervised SimCSE (with only dropout noise) achieves 74.5% Spearman correlation on STS tasks, performing on par with previous supervised methods
all_findings.implication: High-quality sentence embeddings can be learned entirely without labeled data, democratizing NLP for low-resource settings
all_findings.open_question: What is the lower bound of labeled data needed to significantly outperform unsupervised SimCSE?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Unsupervised SimCSE with BERT-base achieves higher STS Spearman correlation than any previous supervised method that does not use NLI data

all_findings.source: SimCSE- Simple Contrastive Learning of Sentence Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: Supervised SimCSE incorporates NLI entailment pairs as positives and contradiction pairs as hard negatives, achieving 81.6% Spearman correlation, a 4.6-point improvement over prior best
all_findings.implication: Natural language inference data provides the ideal supervisory signal for sentence embedding because it captures semantic equivalence and contrast
all_findings.open_question: Can automatically generated NLI-like pairs (e.g., from paraphrasing models) substitute for human-annotated NLI data?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Using NLI entailment pairs as positives and contradiction pairs as hard negatives yields better sentence embeddings than using any other available labeled sentence pair data

all_findings.source: SimCSE- Simple Contrastive Learning of Sentence Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: Contrastive learning theoretically regularizes the anisotropic embedding space of pre-trained language models to be more uniform, which addresses a known failure mode of BERT embeddings
all_findings.implication: The uniformity of the embedding space is a key quality factor; evaluation metrics should include both alignment and uniformity measures
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal trade-off between alignment (similarity of positive pairs) and uniformity (distribution of embeddings) for downstream tasks?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Adding a uniformity regularization term to any sentence embedding method improves performance on semantic textual similarity tasks

all_findings.source: SimCSE- Simple Contrastive Learning of Sentence Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: Supervised signals improve alignment (positive pairs closer together) while unsupervised training primarily improves uniformity (embeddings more evenly distributed)
all_findings.implication: Alignment and uniformity are complementary objectives; different training regimes optimize each differently
all_findings.open_question: Does alignment or uniformity matter more for specific downstream tasks like clustering vs. retrieval vs. classification?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Unsupervised methods (better uniformity) outperform on clustering tasks while supervised methods (better alignment) outperform on classification tasks

all_findings.source: Deep Clustering for Unsupervised Learning of Visual Features.pdf
all_findings.finding: DeepCluster jointly learns neural network parameters and cluster assignments by iteratively k-means clustering features then using assignments as pseudo-labels for supervised training
all_findings.implication: Unsupervised visual feature learning can be framed as alternating between clustering and representation learning, creating a self-supervised loop
all_findings.open_question: How sensitive is DeepCluster to the quality of initial random features and the choice of k (number of clusters)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: DeepCluster's performance improves with more clusters up to the point where cluster purity degrades, showing an optimal k for each dataset

all_findings.source: Deep Clustering for Unsupervised Learning of Visual Features.pdf
all_findings.finding: DeepCluster applied to ImageNet and YFCC100M outperforms prior unsupervised state-of-the-art by a significant margin on all benchmarks
all_findings.implication: Large-scale unsupervised learning can produce visual features competitive with supervised pre-training for transfer learning tasks
all_findings.open_question: Does DeepCluster work as well on non-image domains like text or audio?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: DeepCluster on ImageNet produces features that, when transferred, achieve within 5% of supervised ImageNet pre-training on PASCAL VOC object detection

all_findings.source: Deep Clustering for Unsupervised Learning of Visual Features.pdf
all_findings.finding: The method requires careful handling of trivial solutions (assigning all instances to one cluster) by using a uniform prior over cluster assignments and resampling
all_findings.implication: Unsupervised clustering approaches are prone to degenerate solutions; explicit regularization (uniform cluster prior, equal sampling) is critical
all_findings.open_question: What is the most principled way to prevent degenerate cluster assignments in deep clustering methods?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Without a uniform cluster prior constraint, DeepCluster collapses to assigning >90% of images to a single cluster within 10 epochs

all_findings.source: Deep Clustering for Unsupervised Learning of Visual Features.pdf
all_findings.finding: DeepCluster uses standard data augmentation and an overcomplete architecture, showing that learning visual features without labels can scale to millions of images
all_findings.implication: Label-free representation learning can leverage the ever-increasing availability of unlabeled images at web scale
all_findings.open_question: What is the scaling law for DeepCluster performance as a function of unlabeled dataset size?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Doubling the unlabeled dataset size for DeepCluster training yields a log-linear improvement in downstream task performance

all_findings.source: SCAN- Learning to Classify Images without Labels.pdf
all_findings.finding: SCAN decouples feature learning from clustering: first learn semantically meaningful features via self-supervision, then cluster using these features as a prior
all_findings.implication: End-to-end unsupervised clustering is inferior to a two-stage pipeline; separating representation learning from clustering prevents the model from exploiting low-level features
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal self-supervised pretext task for the feature learning stage of SCAN?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The two-stage SCAN approach outperforms end-to-end deep clustering methods by >20% accuracy on CIFAR10 unsupervised classification

all_findings.source: SCAN- Learning to Classify Images without Labels.pdf
all_findings.finding: SCAN outperforms previous state-of-the-art by massive margins: +26.6% on CIFAR10, +25.0% on CIFAR100-20, +21.3% on STL10 for unsupervised classification accuracy
all_findings.implication: Decoupling feature learning and clustering is a breakthrough strategy for unsupervised image classification, achieving results that approach supervised performance
all_findings.open_question: Can the decoupled approach match fully supervised accuracy on simpler datasets?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: SCAN achieves >90% clustering accuracy on CIFAR10, approaching the ~95% supervised baseline, on STL10 it reaches ~80% vs ~70% for prior methods

all_findings.source: SCAN- Learning to Classify Images without Labels.pdf
all_findings.finding: SCAN is the first unsupervised method to perform well on large-scale ImageNet classification and outperforms several semi-supervised methods in the low-data regime without ground-truth labels
all_findings.implication: Unsupervised classification is now viable for real-world applications where labeled data is scarce but unlabeled images are abundant
all_findings.open_question: How does SCAN's ImageNet performance compare to few-shot learning methods that use a small labeled set?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: SCAN without any labels outperforms semi-supervised methods that use 10% labeled data on ImageNet classification

all_findings.source: SCAN- Learning to Classify Images without Labels.pdf
all_findings.finding: SCAN uses a learnable clustering approach with a prior from self-supervised features, removing the ability for clustering to depend on low-level features
all_findings.implication: Explicitly preventing the clustering stage from relying on low-level visual features (by using frozen high-level representations) forces semantic clustering
all_findings.open_question: What types of self-supervised learning produce the best priors for the clustering stage?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Using contrastive learning (SimCLR/MoCo) as the self-supervised feature backbone yields 5-10% higher clustering accuracy than using rotation prediction or colorization

all_findings.source: Bag of Tricks for Efficient Text Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: fastText uses a linear classifier with n-gram features and a hierarchical softmax, achieving accuracy on par with deep learning classifiers but orders of magnitude faster
all_findings.implication: For many text classification problems, a properly tuned linear model with n-gram features is a sufficient and more practical solution than deep neural networks
all_findings.open_question: What is the exact performance crossover point where deep classifiers meaningfully outperform fastText?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: fastText achieves within 2% of CNN/RNN accuracy on standard text classification benchmarks while being >1000x faster in training time

all_findings.source: Bag of Tricks for Efficient Text Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: With the fastText library, training on >1 billion words takes <10 minutes on a standard multicore CPU, and classifying 500K sentences across 312K classes takes <1 minute
all_findings.implication: fastText enables text classification at web scale without GPU infrastructure, making it ideal for resource-constrained or high-throughput production systems
all_findings.open_question: At what throughput threshold does the latency advantage of fastText become critical for production deployment decisions?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: fastText can classify 1 million documents per second on a single CPU core, enabling real-time text classification at web scale

all_findings.source: Bag of Tricks for Efficient Text Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: The method uses subword n-grams (character n-grams) to capture partial word information, making it effective for morphologically rich languages and misspellings
all_findings.implication: Character-level information in subword units can be captured efficiently without a deep architecture, which is useful for morphologically rich languages
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal n-gram range for fastText across different languages and domains?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Character n-grams (3-6 chars) in fastText improve accuracy on morphologically rich languages by 5-10% compared to word-only models

all_findings.source: Bag of Tricks for Efficient Text Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: Simple linear models with the right features (n-grams) can achieve state-of-the-art performance, and deep learning is not always necessary for text classification
all_findings.implication: Teams should establish a simple linear baseline before investing in complex deep learning architectures for text classification
all_findings.open_question: Which text classification tasks/datasets show the largest gap between fastText and deep learning?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: On datasets with <100K training examples, fastText matches or exceeds deep learning methods, with the gap only appearing at very large scales

all_findings.source: rules_of_ml.pdf
all_findings.finding: Rule #1: Don't be afraid to launch a product without machine learning. Start with simple heuristics before adding ML complexity
all_findings.implication: ML is not always needed; simple rule-based systems can solve many problems and provide a strong baseline for future ML improvements
all_findings.open_question: How do you systematically decide when a problem justifies ML investment vs. remaining heuristic-based?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Teams that deploy a heuristic baseline first achieve faster time-to-market and better ML models later compared to teams that build ML models from the start

all_findings.source: rules_of_ml.pdf
