all_findings.finding: Rule #4: Keep the first model simple and get the infrastructure right. Rule #14: Starting with an interpretable model makes debugging easier
all_findings.implication: First ML models should prioritize simplicity and debuggability over state-of-the-art accuracy; infrastructure for serving, logging, and monitoring is more important than model complexity
all_findings.open_question: At what point should teams transition from simple (logistic regression/GBDT) to complex (deep learning) models?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Teams that start with a simple, interpretable model (e.g., logistic regression) and gradually increase complexity have lower overall project failure rates than teams that start with deep learning

all_findings.source: rules_of_ml.pdf
all_findings.finding: Rule #12: Don't overthink which objective you choose to directly optimize. Rule #13: Choose a simple, observable and attributable metric for your first objective
all_findings.implication: The offline metric should be directly measurable from user behavior, attributable to the model's decisions, and simple enough to reason about
all_findings.open_question: How should teams handle the gap between the proxy metric they optimize and the business metric they care about?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Models optimized for a simple, directly measurable proxy metric (e.g., CTR) outperform models optimized for a complex derived business metric in A/B tests

all_findings.source: rules_of_ml.pdf
all_findings.finding: Rule #16: Plan to launch and iterate. Rule #17: Start with directly observed features, then cross features, then derived features
all_findings.implication: Feature engineering should follow a hierarchy: start with raw observed features before creating crosses or learned features
all_findings.open_question: What is the marginal ROI of each additional feature engineering iteration?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Feature engineering iterations show diminishing returns, with the first 20% of features capturing 80% of the potential performance improvement

all_findings.source: rules_of_ml.pdf
all_findings.finding: Rule #24: When model performance plateaus, look for qualitatively new sources of information rather than refining existing signals
all_findings.implication: Model improvements are often bottlenecked by data, not model architecture. New data sources provide bigger leaps than model tuning
all_findings.open_question: How do teams systematically identify and prioritize new data sources when model performance plateaus?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Adding a qualitatively new data source improves model performance 3x more than spending the same effort on existing signal refinement

all_findings.source: rules_of_ml.pdf
all_findings.finding: Rule #33: When training across multiple teams, consider the data architecture to avoid conflicting objectives and data dependencies
all_findings.implication: Organizational scaling of ML requires data architecture planning to prevent teams from working at cross-purposes
all_findings.open_question: What data governance structures best support multi-team ML development at scale?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Teams sharing a centralized feature store achieve higher model velocity and fewer conflicting changes than teams with decentralized feature ownership

all_findings.source: rules_of_ml.pdf
all_findings.finding: Rule #7: Turn heuristics into features, or handle them externally. If existing rules solve a problem, encode them as features in ML rather than bypassing ML
all_findings.implication: Existing business rules contain valuable signal; they should be incorporated into ML models as features rather than replaced entirely
all_findings.open_question: How should teams prioritize which heuristics to convert to features first?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Incorporating existing rules as features in an ML model outperforms replacing them entirely, with a 10-15% accuracy improvement on edge cases

all_findings.source: 150 Successful Machine Learning Models- 6 Lessons Learned at Booking.pdf
all_findings.finding: An iterative, hypothesis-driven process integrated with business disciplines was fundamental to building 150 successful ML products
all_findings.implication: ML success requires tight integration with product, design, and business teams - not just algorithmic excellence
all_findings.open_question: What organizational structures best support hypothesis-driven ML product development at scale?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Teams using hypothesis-driven development (clear A/B test pre-registration) launch 2x more successful ML models than teams using ad-hoc development

all_findings.source: 150 Successful Machine Learning Models- 6 Lessons Learned at Booking.pdf
all_findings.finding: All 150 models were validated through rigorous Randomized Controlled Trials (A/B tests) exposed to hundreds of millions of users worldwide
all_findings.implication: Online experimentation is the gold standard for ML model validation; offline metrics are proxies that must be validated against online results
all_findings.open_question: What is the correlation between offline metrics and online experiment results across different ML application types at Booking?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: At least 30% of ML models that show offline metric improvement fail to show statistically significant positive online business impact in A/B tests

all_findings.source: 150 Successful Machine Learning Models- 6 Lessons Learned at Booking.pdf
all_findings.finding: Key lessons: (1) Start simple, (2) Focus on data quality over model complexity, (3) Validate offline and online, (4) Monitor for concept drift, (5) Invest in ML infrastructure, (6) Build cross-functional teams
all_findings.implication: The major risks in industrial ML are not algorithmic but operational: data quality, infrastructure, monitoring, and organizational alignment
all_findings.open_question: Which of these 6 lessons is most commonly violated in failed ML projects?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Neglecting data quality monitoring (lesson 2) is the most common failure mode for production ML systems, causing >40% of performance degradations

all_findings.source: 150 Successful Machine Learning Models- 6 Lessons Learned at Booking.pdf
all_findings.finding: Many challenges arose from scaling ML across dozens of teams: need for shared infrastructure, standardized experimentation, and model governance
all_findings.implication: As ML adoption grows, platform-level investments in tooling and standards become as important as individual model improvements
all_findings.open_question: What is the minimum scale at which centralized ML platform investment becomes cost-justified?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Centralized ML infrastructure reduces per-model development time by 40% and increases model success rate by 15% in organizations with >5 ML teams

all_findings.source: 150 Successful Machine Learning Models- 6 Lessons Learned at Booking.pdf
all_findings.finding: The analysis found that successful ML applications focused on clear business problems rather than technical sophistication, with simple models often outperforming complex ones
all_findings.implication: Model selection should be driven by business problem requirements and deployment constraints, not by technical novelty
all_findings.open_question: What fraction of Booking's 150 models could have been equally successful with a simpler algorithm?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Simple models (linear models, GBDTs) account for >70% of successful ML applications at Booking.com despite deep learning receiving more research attention

all_findings.source: Simple Unsupervised Keyphrase Extraction using Sentence Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: EmbedRank uses sentence embeddings (from Sentence-BERT or universal sentence encoders) to embed both the document and candidate phrases, then ranks candidates by cosine similarity to the document
all_findings.implication: Semantic similarity between document and phrase embeddings can replace graph-based ranking for keyphrase extraction, achieving higher accuracy
all_findings.open_question: Which sentence embedding model produces the best keyphrase extraction results?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: EmbedRank with sentence-transformers achieves higher F1 than graph-based TextRank for unsupervised keyphrase extraction

all_findings.source: Simple Unsupervised Keyphrase Extraction using Sentence Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: EmbedRank explicitly increases coverage and diversity among selected keyphrases by introducing an embedding-based Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) criterion
all_findings.implication: Diversity among extracted keyphrases is important for user satisfaction even if F-score does not capture the benefit
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal trade-off between relevance and diversity for keyphrase extraction?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: High-diversity keyphrase selection using embedding-MMR is preferred by human raters over relevance-only selection, even when F-score is unchanged

all_findings.source: Simple Unsupervised Keyphrase Extraction using Sentence Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: EmbedRank is suitable for real-time processing of large amounts of web data, addressing a key limitation of graph-based methods
all_findings.implication: The method is practical for production systems that need per-document keyphrase extraction at scale without a corpus-level graph
all_findings.open_question: What throughput can EmbedRank achieve on a single CPU vs GPU for real-time keyphrase extraction?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: EmbedRank processes documents 100x faster than graph-based methods because it doesn't require constructing a document-level graph for each input

all_findings.source: Simple Unsupervised Keyphrase Extraction using Sentence Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: User study with 200+ votes showed that high-diversity selection (via MMR) is preferred by humans over relevance-only selection
all_findings.implication: Evaluation metrics for keyphrase extraction should incorporate diversity/coverage, not just precision/recall against a ground truth set
all_findings.open_question: How should diversity be incorporated into automated evaluation metrics for keyphrase extraction?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Human preference correlates more strongly with embedding diversity (coverage of semantic space) than with traditional F1 score

all_findings.source: Unsupervised Keyphrase Extraction with Multipartite Graphs.pdf
all_findings.finding: Encodes topical information within a multipartite graph structure where keyphrase candidates and topics are nodes, exploiting their mutually reinforcing relationship
all_findings.implication: Topic information provides a useful signal for keyphrase extraction that is not captured by word-level graphs alone
all_findings.open_question: How does topic granularity (number of topics) affect keyphrase extraction quality?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A multipartite graph with topic nodes outperforms standard TextRank by leveraging the mutually reinforcing relationship between topics and keyphrases

all_findings.source: Unsupervised Keyphrase Extraction with Multipartite Graphs.pdf
all_findings.finding: Novel mechanism to incorporate keyphrase selection preferences (length, POS patterns) into the model
all_findings.implication: Domain-specific preferences for keyphrase form can be directly encoded into the ranking algorithm
all_findings.open_question: How much do selection preferences vary by domain (scientific vs. news vs. web)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Encoding keyphrase length and POS preferences into the ranking model improves precision at the top-K by 10-15% compared to unconstrained ranking

all_findings.source: Unsupervised Keyphrase Extraction with Multipartite Graphs.pdf
all_findings.finding: Significant improvements over state-of-the-art graph-based models on three widely used datasets
all_findings.implication: Graph-based keyphrase extraction still has room for improvement through better graph structure (multipartite) rather than better ranking algorithms
all_findings.open_question: How do multipartite graph approaches compare to embedding-based approaches like EmbedRank?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Multipartite graph approach outperforms TextRank on all three benchmark datasets (Inspec, SemEval2010, DUC2001)

all_findings.source: Key2Vec- Automatic Ranked Keyphrase Extraction from Scientific Articles using Phrase Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: Key2Vec leverages phrase embeddings trained on domain-specific corpora for thematic representation of scientific articles and ranking keyphrases using theme-weighted PageRank
all_findings.implication: Learning distributed representations of phrases (not just words) enables better semantic matching between documents and keyphrase candidates
all_findings.open_question: How large must the domain corpus be to train effective phrase embeddings for keyphrase extraction?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Theme-weighted PageRank using phrase embeddings outperforms standard PageRank for ranked keyphrase extraction from scientific articles

all_findings.source: Key2Vec- Automatic Ranked Keyphrase Extraction from Scientific Articles using Phrase Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: Proposes an effective way of processing text documents for training multi-word phrase embeddings
all_findings.implication: Phrase-level embeddings capture compositional semantics better than aggregating word embeddings for keyphrase extraction
all_findings.open_question: How does phrase embedding quality vary with different training corpora (general vs. domain-specific)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Multi-word phrase embeddings trained via the proposed method capture semantic similarity better than average word embeddings for keyphrase-document matching

all_findings.source: Key2Vec- Automatic Ranked Keyphrase Extraction from Scientific Articles using Phrase Embeddings.pdf
all_findings.finding: Achieves state-of-the-art results on benchmark datasets for keyphrase extraction from scientific articles
all_findings.implication: Unsupervised phrase embedding-based methods can compete with or exceed supervised keyphrase extraction methods
all_findings.open_question: How does Key2Vec perform on non-scientific domains like news or social media?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Key2Vec achieves state-of-the-art F1 on Inspec and SemEval2010 scientific article keyphrase extraction benchmarks

all_findings.source: Deep Keyphrase Generation.pdf
all_findings.finding: Proposes a generative model for keyphrase prediction with an encoder-decoder framework that can generate keyphrases not present in the source text (absent keyphrases)
all_findings.implication: Keyphrase generation (not just extraction) can capture deeper semantic meaning and produce novel phrases that summarize document content
all_findings.open_question: How should the quality of absent keyphrases (phrases not in the text) be evaluated?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: An encoder-decoder model for keyphrase generation outperforms extraction-only methods on both present and absent keyphrase F1@M metrics

all_findings.source: Deep Keyphrase Generation.pdf
all_findings.finding: The sequence-to-sequence keyphrase generation approach overcomes the limitation of extraction methods that cannot identify keyphrases not appearing verbatim in the text
all_findings.implication: For tasks requiring abstractive summarization-like keyphrases, generative approaches are necessary despite being harder to train
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal ratio of present vs. absent keyphrases for different applications?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Generative keyphrase models produce more diverse and semantically representative keyphrases than extraction models, especially for long documents

all_findings.source: Keyphrase Generation with Correlation Constraints.pdf
all_findings.finding: CorrRNN captures correlation among multiple keyphrases using a coverage vector (to track what has been summarized) and preceding phrase context (to eliminate duplicates)
all_findings.implication: Keyphrases should not be generated independently; correlation and coverage constraints improve diversity and reduce redundancy
all_findings.open_question: How does the coverage mechanism interact with document length and keyphrase count?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Adding coverage and correlation constraints to keyphrase generation reduces duplicate keyphrases by >50% while improving overall F1

all_findings.source: Keyphrase Generation with Correlation Constraints.pdf
all_findings.finding: The model significantly outperforms state-of-the-art on benchmark datasets in both accuracy and diversity
all_findings.implication: Explicitly modeling inter-keyphrase dependencies is crucial for high-quality multi-keyphrase generation
all_findings.open_question: Can correlation constraints be applied to extraction methods or only to generation methods?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: CorrRNN with coverage and correlation constraints produces more diverse keyphrase sets (higher distinct n-gram ratio) than models without these constraints

all_findings.source: Semi-Supervised Learning for Neural Keyphrase Generation.pdf
all_findings.finding: Semi-supervised keyphrase generation using unlabeled data with synthetic keyphrases from unsupervised extraction methods or self-learning improves over supervised-only training
all_findings.implication: Large amounts of unlabeled documents can be leveraged to improve keyphrase generation in low-resource domains
all_findings.open_question: What quality threshold must synthetic keyphrases meet to provide benefit rather than noise?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Self-training with iteratively refined pseudo-keyphrases improves keyphrase generation F1 by >5% over supervised-only baselines

all_findings.source: Semi-Supervised Learning for Neural Keyphrase Generation.pdf
all_findings.finding: Multi-task learning framework jointly learns keyphrase generation and title generation, sharing representations between the two related tasks
all_findings.implication: Related generation tasks can provide complementary supervision; a single model can produce both keyphrases and titles with mutual benefit
all_findings.open_question: What other auxiliary tasks could benefit keyphrase generation via multi-task learning?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Jointly training for keyphrase generation and title generation yields better performance on both tasks than training them separately

all_findings.source: Learning Classifiers from Only Positive and Unlabeled Data.pdf
all_findings.finding: Under the assumption that labeled examples are selected randomly from positive examples, a classifier trained on positive and unlabeled data predicts probabilities that differ by only a constant factor from true conditional probabilities
all_findings.implication: PU learning is theoretically grounded when the labeled positives are a random subset of all positives; the constant factor can be estimated or treated as a threshold
all_findings.open_question: How robust is the method to violations of the 'selected completely at random' assumption?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A PU classifier trained under the SCAR assumption predicts rankings identical to a fully supervised classifier, differing only in the probability calibration

all_findings.source: Learning Classifiers from Only Positive and Unlabeled Data.pdf
all_findings.finding: Two practical methods for learning from positive and unlabeled data: (1) treat unlabeled as negative with weighted loss, (2) use a two-step approach where you identify reliable negatives first
all_findings.implication: PU learning can be implemented via simple modification to standard classifiers, making it accessible for practical deployment
all_findings.open_question: Which of the two proposed methods is more robust to noise in the positive-labeled data?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The two-step approach (identify reliable negatives first) outperforms weighted loss methods when the labeled positive set is incomplete

all_findings.source: Learning Classifiers from Only Positive and Unlabeled Data.pdf
all_findings.finding: Models trained using the new PU methods outperform the biased SVM method for learning from positive and unlabeled examples on a real-world protein database task
all_findings.implication: PU learning methods have practical utility in scientific databases where only positive examples are curated
all_findings.open_question: How do PU methods compare to one-class classification methods for anomaly detection tasks?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: PU learning methods produce more accurate classifiers than biased SVM when only <10% of positive examples are labeled

all_findings.source: Learning Classifiers from Only Positive and Unlabeled Data.pdf
all_findings.finding: The key insight is that the probability of being positive given unlabeled status can be expressed in terms of the true positive class prior and the labeling propensity
all_findings.implication: Estimating the positive class prior (proportion of positives in unlabeled data) is a critical preprocessing step for PU learning
all_findings.open_question: What is the most robust method for estimating the class prior in PU settings?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Misestimating the positive class prior by >10% leads to >5% degradation in PU classifier F1 score

all_findings.source: Real-time Attention Based Look-alike Model for Recommender System.pdf
all_findings.finding: RALM addresses the 'Matthew effect' in recommender systems - head contents get more popular while long-tail contents lack exposure due to behavior feature sparsity
all_findings.implication: Look-alike models can promote content diversity by expanding audiences for long-tail items that lack behavioral features
all_findings.open_question: At what level of content popularity does look-alike audience expansion become most impactful?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: RALM increases exposure of long-tail content by >30% while maintaining overall recommendation quality

all_findings.source: Real-time Attention Based Look-alike Model for Recommender System.pdf
all_findings.finding: Attention merge layer replaces concatenation layer in neural networks, significantly improving expressive ability for multi-field feature learning
all_findings.implication: Attention-based feature interaction can replace simple concatenation for better user representation learning
all_findings.open_question: How does attention merge compare to other feature interaction methods like FM or DCN?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Attention merge layer improves recommendation AUC by 2-3% over concatenation layer for multi-field user features

all_findings.source: Real-time Attention Based Look-alike Model for Recommender System.pdf
all_findings.finding: Global attention unit and local attention units model the various members of seed users differently, capturing both shared and query-specific patterns
all_findings.implication: Different attention mechanisms at different levels (global vs local) capture complementary aspects of seed-user similarity
all_findings.open_question: How many local attention units are optimal for capturing diverse seed user patterns?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Dual attention (global + local) outperforms single attention mechanisms for look-alike modeling because it captures both seed-set-wide and per-seed patterns

all_findings.source: Real-time Attention Based Look-alike Model for Recommender System.pdf
all_findings.finding: RALM achieves real-time look-alike audience extension through seeds-to-user similarity prediction, solving the conflict between real-time requirements and model effectiveness
all_findings.implication: Pre-computing user embeddings and computing seed-to-user similarity in real-time enables practical look-alike at scale
all_findings.open_question: What is the latency budget for real-time look-alike inference in a production recommender system?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: RALM achieves sub-10ms inference time per seed-user pair, enabling real-time look-alike scoring at recommender system scale

all_findings.source: Deep One-Class Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: Deep SVDD (Support Vector Data Description) is a new anomaly detection method trained on an anomaly-detection-based objective, unlike previous methods that adapted other tasks
all_findings.implication: Anomaly detection benefits from being trained as an anomaly detection objective rather than being a byproduct of other training objectives
all_findings.open_question: How does Deep SVDD compare to other deep one-class methods on high-dimensional data like text or time series?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Deep SVDD trained with an anomaly detection objective outperforms autoencoder-based anomaly detection (reconstruction error) on MNIST and CIFAR-10

all_findings.source: Deep One-Class Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: The neural network and training procedure must satisfy certain properties (no bias terms, bounded activation functions) to prevent trivial solutions (mapping all data to the origin)
all_findings.implication: Deep one-class methods are prone to collapse; architectural constraints are necessary to prevent degenerate solutions
all_findings.open_question: What architectural constraints are theoretically necessary vs. empirically helpful for deep one-class methods?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Without removing bias terms, Deep SVDD collapses to a trivial solution where all data points are mapped to the same point in the output space

all_findings.source: Audience Expansion for Online Social Network Advertising.pdf
all_findings.finding: LinkedIn developed two audience expansion methods: campaign-agnostic expansion (general similarity model) and campaign-aware expansion (per-campaign models), with a hybrid strategy combining both
all_findings.implication: A hybrid approach balances the scalability of campaign-agnostic methods with the precision of campaign-aware methods
all_findings.open_question: What fraction of campaigns benefit from the hybrid strategy vs. either pure approach?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Hybrid audience expansion (combining campaign-agnostic and campaign-aware) achieves higher reach at the same precision than either method alone

all_findings.source: Audience Expansion for Online Social Network Advertising.pdf
all_findings.finding: Audience expansion simplifies targeting for advertisers and increases market efficiency by better utilizing ad inventory
all_findings.implication: Look-alike modeling creates a win-win: advertisers reach more relevant users and platforms monetize more inventory
all_findings.open_question: How does look-alike expansion affect user experience and ad relevance perception?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Audience expansion increases advertiser reach by >50% while maintaining or improving click-through rates

all_findings.source: Finding Users Who Act Alike- Transfer Learning for Expanding Advertiser Audiences.pdf
all_findings.finding: Two-stage embedding-based audience expansion: (1) global user embedding model trained on sitewide activity logs, (2) lightweight seed list representation using transfer learning and statistical techniques
all_findings.implication: A single shared user embedding space can serve multiple advertisers, with lightweight per-advertiser adaptations rather than full model training per campaign
all_findings.open_question: How should the global user embedding model be updated to capture changing user behavior while maintaining stable embeddings for existing campaigns?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Transfer learning from a global user embedding model requires 100x less data per advertiser than training a campaign-specific model from scratch

all_findings.source: Finding Users Who Act Alike- Transfer Learning for Expanding Advertiser Audiences.pdf
all_findings.finding: Ensemble approach combining single-advertiser classifiers with the embedding-based technique produces the best results across all seed list sizes
all_findings.implication: Ensembling different look-alike approaches (embedding similarity + per-advertiser classifier) gives the most robust audience expansion
all_findings.open_question: How should the ensemble weights be tuned when seed lists vary dramatically in size across advertisers?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: An ensemble of embedding-based similarity and per-advertiser classifiers outperforms either approach alone for audience expansion at Pinterest

all_findings.source: Hubble- An Industrial System for Audience Expansion in Mobile Marketing.pdf
all_findings.finding: Two-stage audience expansion: offline heavyweight user representation learning and online embedding-based lightweight audience expansion, addressing scalability and timeliness
all_findings.implication: Separation of offline representation learning from online serving enables industrial-scale audience expansion
all_findings.open_question: How often should the offline user representations be refreshed to capture changing user behavior?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A two-stage approach (offline embeddings + online expansion) handles 1000x more campaigns than per-campaign training approaches

all_findings.source: Hubble- An Industrial System for Audience Expansion in Mobile Marketing.pdf
all_findings.finding: Conventional two-stage systems neglect high-order user-campaign interactions and generate entangled user embeddings, failing to achieve high-quality representations
all_findings.implication: User embeddings must capture campaign-relevant information rather than being a general-purpose representation
all_findings.open_question: What is the best method to ensure user embeddings are disentangled rather than entangled for different campaign types?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: User embeddings that model user-campaign interaction information outperform general-purpose user embeddings for audience expansion

all_findings.source: Hubble- An Industrial System for Audience Expansion in Mobile Marketing.pdf
all_findings.finding: Seed users provided by experts or collected from user feedback could be noisy and fail to cover actual audiences, introducing coverage bias
all_findings.implication: Quality and coverage of seed users significantly impacts look-alike model performance; seed selection strategies matter
all_findings.open_question: How should noisy seeds be detected and filtered automatically?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Noisy seed users (false positives in the seed set) disproportionately degrade look-alike model precision by >15% when >5% of seeds are noisy

all_findings.source: A Sub-linear, Massive-scale Look-alike Audience Extension System.pdf
all_findings.finding: Graph-based look-alike method with nearest-neighbor filtering outperforms other methods by >50% regarding conversion rate in app-install ad campaigns
all_findings.implication: Graph-based approaches leveraging user-user similarity graphs can dramatically outperform feature-based approaches for look-alike modeling
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal graph construction method (similarity metric, neighborhood size) for look-alike modeling?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Graph-based nearest-neighbor look-alike achieves >50% higher conversion rate than feature-based logistic regression for app-install campaigns

all_findings.source: A Sub-linear, Massive-scale Look-alike Audience Extension System.pdf
all_findings.finding: The system operates at sub-linear complexity, making it feasible for billions of users and millions of features
all_findings.implication: Sub-linear scalability is essential for look-alike systems to operate at internet scale
all_findings.open_question: How does the sub-linear system performance degrade as the user base grows to 10x current scale?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The sub-linear look-alike system maintains <100ms query latency even with billions of users through nearest-neighbor approximation techniques

all_findings.source: Adaptive look-alike targeting in social networks advertising.pdf
all_findings.finding: Adaptive look-alike model adjusts to different campaign requirements by using context-aware similarity measures
all_findings.implication: A single one-size-fits-all similarity function is insufficient for diverse advertising campaigns; context adaptation is needed
all_findings.open_question: What contextual signals (campaign type, industry, budget) most influence optimal look-alike strategy?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Adaptive look-alike targeting (context-dependent) outperforms static similarity models by adapting to campaign-specific characteristics

all_findings.source: Attention Is All You Need.pdf
all_findings.finding: The Transformer architecture based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely, achieves superior quality on machine translation while being more parallelizable
all_findings.implication: Attention mechanisms alone are sufficient for sequence modeling; recurrence is not necessary for state-of-the-art performance
all_findings.open_question: What are the fundamental limits of attention-only architectures for sequence tasks of varying lengths?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Transformer without recurrence achieves 28.4 BLEU on WMT 2014 En-De translation, improving over all previous recurrent/convolutional models

all_findings.source: Attention Is All You Need.pdf
all_findings.finding: Multi-head attention allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions
all_findings.implication: Multiple attention heads capture different types of relationships (syntactic, semantic, positional) in parallel
all_findings.open_question: How does the number of attention heads affect model capacity vs. computational cost?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Increasing attention heads improves translation quality up to a point (8-16 heads), after which gains diminish

all_findings.source: Attention Is All You Need.pdf
all_findings.finding: Positional encodings are necessary since the model has no recurrence; the paper uses sinusoidal position encodings that allow the model to extrapolate to longer sequences
all_findings.implication: Position information must be explicitly injected into attention-based models, and sinusoidal encodings provide a useful inductive bias
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal positional encoding scheme for different types of sequence data?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Sinusoidal positional encodings allow Transformers to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training

all_findings.source: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale.pdf
all_findings.finding: A pure Transformer (ViT) applied directly to sequences of image patches performs very well on image classification, showing that CNN reliance is not necessary for vision tasks
all_findings.implication: The same architecture (Transformer) can process both language and vision, opening the door to unified multimodal models
all_findings.open_question: What is the minimal patch size and pre-training dataset size for ViT to match CNN performance?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: ViT pre-trained on large datasets (ImageNet-21K, JFT-300M) achieves competitive or superior accuracy to SOTA CNNs on ImageNet

all_findings.source: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale.pdf
all_findings.finding: When pre-trained on large amounts of data and transferred to smaller benchmarks, ViT attains excellent results while requiring substantially fewer computational resources to train
all_findings.implication: Vision Transformers are data-hungry but computationally efficient at training time when sufficient data is available
all_findings.open_question: Is there a similar scaling law for ViT as language Transformers where performance improves log-linearly with compute?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: ViT performance scales with pre-training dataset size; on datasets smaller than ImageNet-1M, ViT underperforms ResNet, but on larger datasets it outperforms

all_findings.source: Universal Sentence Encoder.pdf
all_findings.finding: Sentence embedding models designed specifically for transfer learning to other NLP tasks achieve accurate performance on diverse transfer tasks with minimal supervised data
all_findings.implication: Universal sentence encoders can serve as a general-purpose feature extractor for NLP, enabling few-shot and zero-shot transfer
all_findings.open_question: What is the minimal amount of labeled data required for a transfer task when using universal sentence encoders?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Universal sentence encoders achieve good performance on downstream tasks with as few as 100 labeled examples per class

all_findings.source: Universal Sentence Encoder.pdf
all_findings.finding: Two variants of the encoding models allow trade-offs between accuracy and compute resources; transfer using sentence embeddings outperforms word-level transfer
all_findings.implication: Sentence-level transfer is more effective than word-level (word embedding) transfer for most NLP tasks

all_findings.source: Siamese Neural Networks for One-shot Image Recognition.pdf
all_findings.finding: Siamese networks learn a ranking similarity metric between inputs, enabling generalization to entirely new classes from unknown distributions given only one example per class
all_findings.implication: Metric learning via siamese architecture enables one-shot learning by comparing new examples to reference examples in embedding space
all_findings.open_question: How does the siamese architecture compare to triplet networks and prototypical networks for few-shot learning?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Siamese networks achieve near state-of-the-art one-shot classification by learning discriminative features that generalize to unseen classes

all_findings.source: Self-training with Noisy Student improves ImageNet classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: Noisy Student Training extends self-training with equal-or-larger student models and noise (dropout, stochastic depth, RandAugment) injected during student learning
all_findings.implication: Adding noise during self-training forces the student to generalize better than the teacher, enabling semi-supervised learning that exceeds supervised-only performance even when labeled data is abundant
all_findings.open_question: What types and amounts of noise are optimal for the student model?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Noisy Student with 300M unlabeled images achieves 88.4% ImageNet top-1 accuracy, improving 2.0% over the best model trained with 3.5B weakly labeled Instagram images

all_findings.source: Self-training with Noisy Student improves ImageNet classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: Noisy Student improves robustness: ImageNet-A accuracy from 61.0% to 83.7%, ImageNet-C error from 45.7 to 28.3, ImageNet-P flip rate from 27.8 to 12.2
all_findings.implication: Self-training with noise not only improves accuracy but drastically improves model robustness to distribution shifts and corruptions

all_findings.source: SentAugment.pdf
all_findings.finding: SentAugment computes task-specific query embeddings from labeled data to retrieve relevant sentences from billions of unlabeled web sentences for self-training
all_findings.implication: In-domain unlabeled data is not required for semi-supervised NLP; relevant sentences can be retrieved from general web corpora
all_findings.open_question: How task-specific must the query embeddings be for effective augmentation?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: SentAugment improves RoBERTa by up to 2.6% on text classification benchmarks through self-training on retrieved web sentences

all_findings.source: SentAugment.pdf
all_findings.finding: Self-training is complementary to strong RoBERTa baselines, showing improvements of up to 2.6% on standard text classification benchmarks
all_findings.implication: Even well-pre-trained models like RoBERTa benefit from additional semi-supervised self-training with relevant data

all_findings.source: Deep Unordered Composition Rivals Syntactic Methods for Text Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: A deep bag-of-words neural network (without word order or syntax) competes with and sometimes outperforms syntactic models on sentiment and QA tasks
all_findings.implication: Nonlinear transformation of input (deepening the network) is more important than incorporating word order and syntax for many text classification tasks
all_findings.open_question: For which tasks and datasets does word order become critical beyond the performance of deep unordered models?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Deep BOW models with dropout variants match or exceed syntactic neural models on sentiment analysis while training an order of magnitude faster

all_findings.source: Distributed Representations of Sentences and Documents.pdf
all_findings.finding: Paragraph Vector (Doc2Vec) learns fixed-length feature representations from variable-length texts by training to predict words in the document, overcoming BOW limitations
all_findings.implication: Unsupervised document embeddings can capture word order and semantics that bag-of-words misses, improving document classification and clustering
all_findings.open_question: How does Paragraph Vector compare to modern contextual embeddings like BERT for document-level tasks?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Paragraph Vectors outperform bag-of-words features on sentiment analysis and text classification benchmarks

all_findings.source: A Deep Relevance Matching Model for Ad-hoc Retrieval.pdf
all_findings.finding: DRMM identifies fundamental differences between relevance matching (ad-hoc retrieval) and semantic matching (NLP tasks): need for exact matching, query term importance, and diverse matching requirements
all_findings.implication: Information retrieval models should be designed differently from semantic matching models; relevance matching requires different architectural choices
all_findings.open_question: How do these differences manifest in modern neural IR systems that use pre-trained language models?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: DRMM with matching histogram mapping outperforms semantic matching models on ad-hoc retrieval tasks by properly handling exact match signals

all_findings.source: A Deep Relevance Matching Model for Ad-hoc Retrieval.pdf
all_findings.finding: Joint deep architecture at query term level with matching histogram mapping, feed forward matching network, and term gating network effectively handles relevance matching
all_findings.implication: Query term-level processing (rather than full sentence matching) is more appropriate for relevance matching in search

all_findings.source: COLD- Towards the Next Generation of Pre-Ranking System.pdf
all_findings.finding: COLD rethinks pre-ranking from algorithm-system co-design view: jointly optimize the pre-ranking model and the computing power it costs
all_findings.implication: Pre-ranking models need not be simplified versions of ranking models; with compute-aware optimization, complex models with cross features can be used in pre-ranking
all_findings.open_question: How does the COLD approach generalize to other latency-sensitive stages like retrieval?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: COLD with arbitrary deep models and cross features outperforms SOTA vector-product pre-ranking while maintaining the same compute budget

all_findings.source: Bag of Tricks for Efficient Text Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: fastText uses hierarchical softmax for efficient training with large output spaces (312K classes), enabling classification of 500K sentences across 312K classes in <1 minute
all_findings.implication: Hierarchical softmax is a practical necessity for extreme multi-class text classification; flat softmax becomes prohibitively expensive at this scale
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal tree structure for hierarchical softmax given class distribution skew?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: fastText with hierarchical softmax trains >100x faster than flat softmax for problems with >100K classes

all_findings.source: Estimating Training Data Influence by Tracing Gradient Descent.pdf
all_findings.finding: TracIn computes training example influence on a prediction by tracing how the loss on the test point changes during training whenever the training example was utilized
all_findings.implication: Training data quality can be systematically assessed by measuring individual example influence, enabling data debugging and cleaning
all_findings.open_question: How does TracIn scale to billion-example datasets and trillion-parameter models?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: TracIn identifies mislabeled training examples with higher precision than random sampling, enabling targeted data cleaning that improves model accuracy

all_findings.source: Estimating Training Data Influence by Tracing Gradient Descent.pdf
all_findings.finding: TracIn is scalable via first-order gradient approximation, saved checkpoints, and cherry-picking layers, and applies to any model trained with SGD
all_findings.implication: Influence estimation is practical for real-world deep learning workflows without requiring model retraining

all_findings.source: Deep Clustering for Unsupervised Learning of Visual Features.pdf
all_findings.finding: DeepCluster uses a 'pretext-free' approach (unlike jigsaw, rotation, colorization) that avoids hand-crafted pretext tasks, instead clustering directly on learned features
all_findings.implication: Avoiding hand-designed pretext tasks means DeepCluster can potentially generalize to any data modality where k-means is applicable
all_findings.open_question: Can DeepCluster be applied to non-visual modalities like text or audio without modification?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: DeepCluster with appropriate architecture (e.g., Transformer for text) can learn unsupervised representations from text comparable to BERT pre-training

all_findings.source: SCAN- Learning to Classify Images without Labels.pdf
all_findings.finding: SCAN uses a self-supervised task (rotation prediction or contrastive learning) as a prior for clustering, removing the ability for cluster learning to depend on low-level features
all_findings.implication: Self-supervised features provide a semantic prior that prevents the clustering stage from exploiting spurious low-level correlations
all_findings.open_question: How does the choice of self-supervised task affect the quality of the clustering prior across different datasets?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Different self-supervised pretext tasks produce different quality priors for clustering; contrastive methods (SimCLR) produce better priors than rotation prediction

all_findings.source: Applying Deep Learning To Airbnb Search .pdf
all_findings.finding: Transitioning from GBDT to neural networks for search ranking required overcoming a plateau in gains; deep learning was a 'steep learning' that eventually broke the plateau
all_findings.implication: GBDT models plateau after initial large gains; neural networks can break through the plateau but require significant engineering investment
all_findings.open_question: When should teams transition from GBDT to neural networks for ranking vs. continuing to improve GBDT?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Neural network ranking models at Airbnb outperformed the plateaued GBDT model, driving one of the largest improvements in bookings after the initial GBDT launch

all_findings.source: Improving Deep Learning For Airbnb Search .pdf
all_findings.finding: Three key improvements beyond initial DNN deployment: Architecture (new ranking DNN beyond fully connected two-layer), Bias (novel positional bias handling), Cold start (better treatment of new listings)
all_findings.implication: Post-deployment improvement of DNNs requires systematic attention to architecture, bias handling, and cold start - what they call the 'ABCs' of search improvement
all_findings.open_question: How generalizable is the ABC framework to other recommendation and search systems?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Handling positional bias in ranking through the described novel approach led to one of the most significant improvements in tackling inventory the DNN historically found challenging

all_findings.source: EENMF- An End-to-End Neural Matching Framework for E-Commerce Sponsored Search .pdf
all_findings.finding: End-to-end neural matching framework with vector-based ad retrieval (no keyword bidding needed) and neural network-based ad pre-ranking overcomes keyword sparsity for long-tail queries
all_findings.implication: Vector-based retrieval can eliminate the keyword bidding bottleneck in sponsored search, especially for long-tail queries where advertisers don't bid
all_findings.open_question: How does vector-based ad retrieval compare to keyword-based retrieval in terms of ad relevance and platform revenue?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Vector-based ad retrieval improves ad coverage for long-tail queries by >50% compared to keyword-based retrieval

all_findings.source: Time-Series Anomaly Detection Service at Microsoft.pdf
all_findings.finding: First work to borrow Spectral Residual (SR) model from visual saliency detection to time-series anomaly detection, combined with CNN for improved performance
all_findings.implication: Cross-domain transfer of methods (saliency detection -> anomaly detection) can yield novel and effective algorithms
all_findings.open_question: What other cross-domain analogies exist between visual saliency and time-series analysis?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Spectral Residual + CNN anomaly detection outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on both public datasets and Microsoft production data

all_findings.source: Bag of Tricks for Efficient Text Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: fastText uses subword information (character n-grams) to represent words, making it effective for rare words and morphologically rich languages while maintaining speed advantages
all_findings.implication: Subword information can be incorporated efficiently without sacrificing the speed of linear classifiers
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal range of n-gram lengths for different languages?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Character n-grams improve word representation quality for morphologically rich languages (e.g., Finnish, Turkish) compared to word-only models

all_findings.source: Correlation Networks for Extreme Multi-label Text Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: CorNet module learns label correlations and enhances raw label predictions with correlation knowledge, outputting augmented predictions at the final layer
all_findings.implication: Explicitly modeling label correlations is beneficial for extreme multi-label classification, a factor previously ignored by deep XMTC models
all_findings.open_question: How does CorNet's effectiveness change as the label set grows (100K+ labels)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: CorNet brings significant improvements over deep XMTC models in both performance and convergence rate by leveraging label correlation information

all_findings.source: Self-training with Noisy Student improves ImageNet classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: The approach iterates by putting back the student as the teacher, enabling multiple rounds of self-training with progressively better teachers
all_findings.implication: Self-training can be iterated multiple times, with each iteration producing a better teacher for the next round
all_findings.open_question: How many iterations of self-training yield diminishing returns?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Each iteration of Noisy Student training improves ImageNet top-1 accuracy, with successive iterations showing diminishing marginal gains

all_findings.source: Deep One-Class Classification.pdf
all_findings.finding: Deep SVDD is effective on MNIST and CIFAR-10 anomaly detection benchmarks as well as detecting adversarial examples of GTSRB stop signs
all_findings.implication: Deep one-class classification has practical applications beyond novelty detection, including adversarial example detection
all_findings.open_question: How well does Deep SVDD generalize to detecting adversarial examples across different attack methods?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Deep SVDD can distinguish adversarial examples from normal examples with >95% AUC on standard benchmark datasets

all_findings.source: Learning to Cluster Documents into Workspaces Using Large Scale Activity Logs .pdf
all_findings.finding: Co-access signals (whether a user accessed two documents around the same time) are very effective for measuring document relatedness in clustering
all_findings.implication: User activity co-access patterns provide a rich signal for document clustering that goes beyond textual similarity
all_findings.open_question: What is the optimal time window for defining co-access signals?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Co-access-based document clustering outperforms text-similarity-based clustering for workspace suggestion tasks by >20% in user acceptance

all_findings.source: Learning to Cluster Documents into Workspaces Using Large Scale Activity Logs .pdf
all_findings.finding: Weakly supervised clustering using co-access labels achieves comparable or better performance than models trained with human labels
all_findings.implication: Weak supervision from user activity logs can replace expensive human annotations for document clustering tasks
all_findings.open_question: How much weak label noise can the model tolerate before performance degrades below the human-label baseline?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Weakly supervised methods from co-access data achieve comparable performance to human-labeled methods for document clustering in Google Drive

all_findings.source: Learning to Create Better Ads- Generation and Ranking Approaches for Ad Creative Refinement.pdf
all_findings.finding: Encoder-decoder architecture with copy mechanism generates new ad text by copying some words from inferior input text while incorporating new words associated with higher CTR
all_findings.implication: Ad creative refinement can be formulated as a conditional generation task where parts of the source text are preserved and parts are replaced
all_findings.open_question: How does the improvement in expected CTR translate to actual revenue lift in production A/B tests?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Copy mechanism in encoder-decoder models enables generating ad text that preserves brand-relevant terms while improving predicted CTR

all_findings.source: Improving Recommendation Quality in Google Drive.pdf
all_findings.finding: Quick Access cut the amount of time users spend locating files in half, saving ~1000 work weeks every day across ~1 billion users
all_findings.implication: Personal recommendation systems at scale can have massive cumulative impact; even small per-user improvements multiply across billions of users
all_findings.open_question: How do recommendation quality improvements in personal productivity tools differ from media/content recommendations?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Deep network architectures improve Quick Access recommendation quality over heuristic baselines, as measured by user time saved

all_findings.source: Improving Recommendation Quality in Google Drive.pdf
all_findings.finding: Data augmentation and effects of latency and biases in UX were critical success factors alongside model architecture improvements
all_findings.implication: Model improvements alone are insufficient; UX considerations (latency, bias) and data quality are equally important for recommendation systems

all_findings.source: Optuna- A Next-generation Hyperparameter Optimization Framework.pdf
all_findings.finding: Define-by-run API allows dynamic construction of the parameter search space, a novel approach compared to define-and-run in traditional HPO frameworks
all_findings.implication: HPO frameworks should support dynamic search space construction, enabling conditional parameter spaces and iterative refinement
all_findings.open_question: How does define-by-run affect the reproducibility of hyperparameter optimization studies?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Define-by-run API enables more flexible and intuitive hyperparameter optimization than define-and-run approaches

all_findings.source: Robust De-anonymization of Large Sparse Datasets.pdf
all_findings.finding: Statistical de-anonymization attacks against high-dimensional micro-data are robust to perturbation and can identify Netflix subscriber records using IMDB as background knowledge
all_findings.implication: Publishing anonymized user behavior data carries significant privacy risks even after removing identifiers, as records can be re-identified using auxiliary data
all_findings.open_question: What differential privacy guarantees are needed to prevent these de-anonymization attacks?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Netflix Prize dataset can be de-anonymized with high confidence using only IMDB ratings as background knowledge, revealing sensitive user information

all_findings.source: How to Train BERT with an Academic Budget.pdf
all_findings.finding: Recipe for training BERT-like model in 24 hours using only 8 low-range 12GB GPUs through software optimizations, design choices, and hyperparameter tuning
all_findings.implication: Large language model pre-training is accessible to academic groups with modest hardware through careful optimization, not just well-funded industry labs
all_findings.open_question: What is the minimum compute budget required to train a competitive BERT-base model?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Combining faster implementation, overparameterization, single-sequence training, and tuned schedule produces BERTBASE-competitive models at a fraction of the original cost

all_findings.source: ajibm_2123854.pdf
all_findings.finding: The 'Review to Pain Matrix' workflow integrates product discovery and delivery into a single automated pipeline: text cleaning → deduplication (MinHash/SimHash) → thematization → aspect-based sentiment analysis via LLMs → canonical pain formulation → weighted priority scoring (frequency × intensity × impact / effort) → CJM-stage and audience-segment mapping → CustDev loop closure.
all_findings.implication: This provides a systematic, reproducible methodology for transforming unstructured customer feedback into prioritized, actionable product decisions. The Pain Matrix format standardizes the output across analysis waves, enabling quantitative tracking of pain-point evolution and release impact assessment.
all_findings.open_question: How should the weighting coefficients for priority scoring be tuned per product and stage? Is there a universal default weighting that works across industries?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A Pain Matrix with priority scores computed as a weighted combination of frequency, intensity, impact, and effort will better predict which issues get solved and improve product metrics than prioritization based on frequency alone.

all_findings.source: ajibm_2123854.pdf
all_findings.finding: The case study on IT-career-change content analyzed YouTube comments, Telegram posts, and forums for office workers (27-45, burned out, career-stagnant). Three top pains emerged: 'no job guarantee after course' (highest frequency + intensity), 'which language to learn first', and 'can I get into IT after 30/40'. The canonical formulation 'fear of unemployment after course completion due to age or lack of experience' was validated through simulated CustDev dialogues.
all_findings.implication: The methodology successfully identified that the core need is employability assurance, not skill acquisition. This insight led to redesigned marketing emphasizing a 'career track' and mature-graduate success stories, which measurably increased conversion rates.
all_findings.open_question: How does the Pain Matrix compare quantitatively with traditional survey-based needs assessment in terms of speed, cost, and actionable insight quality?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Marketing messages redesigned around Pain Matrix outputs (address top-3 canonical pains) will achieve higher conversion rates than messages based on product-feature-focused copy, in A/B tests across 3 different product categories.

all_findings.source: ajibm_2123854.pdf
all_findings.finding: The pipeline addresses key failure risks: sampling bias (mitigated via multi-platform collection and segment-by-source analysis), machine translation distortion (preserving original + translated text for back-reference), and annotation drift (versioned canonical dictionary, inter-annotator agreement monitoring, drift monitoring across waves).
all_findings.implication: Automated pain-point analysis requires systematic quality control mechanisms to maintain reliability over time. The human-in-the-loop approach (sampling verification, inter-annotator checks, drift monitoring) is essential for production use, even with LLM-based automation.
all_findings.open_question: What is the minimum sample size for manual verification per wave to maintain quality while minimizing human effort? Can quality metrics (inter-annotator agreement, drift scores) trigger automated re-training or alerting?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Verifying 5% of reviews per wave (sampled stratified by source and sentiment) is sufficient to maintain <5% annotation drift across 10 consecutive waves, compared to verifying 20%.

all_findings.source: ajibm_2123854.pdf
all_findings.finding: The storage architecture uses a multi-layer approach: Google Docs for wave reports, Google Sheets for the master Pain Matrix (with Reviews, Aspects, PainsCanon, Scores, RoadmapLinks sheets), Notion for long-term knowledge base (segment cards, hypotheses, avatars), and Miro/FigJam for visual maps. Automation spans no-code (CSV+Sheets+Apps Script) to advanced (embeddings + semantic clustering + topic drift detection).
all_findings.implication: A practical production system for pain-point intelligence can be built with accessible, low-cost tools and incrementally upgraded. The key architectural insight is maintaining traceability from raw review → aspect → canonical pain → priority score → roadmap item, enabling full auditability of decisions.
all_findings.open_question: How well does this tooling stack scale to thousands of reviews per day? What are the bottlenecks (Sheets row limits, API costs, embedding computational requirements)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The proposed multi-sheet schema can support analysis of up to 100K reviews per wave without performance degradation, with the Reviews sheet being the primary bottleneck beyond which a database backend is required.

all_findings.source: applsci-10-08473-v2.pdf
all_findings.finding: The Context Tree and HSCS (Hierarchical Search of Concept Spaces) algorithm provide a quantitative framework for identifying customers' unmet needs from online social media (Twitter, blog posts) by iteratively expanding keyword-based concept spaces. The tree structure summarizes user experiences around target products (refrigerator, washing machine, robot cleaner, air conditioner, oven) through co-occurring related keywords and their corresponding document sets.
all_findings.implication: This enables structured, hierarchical exploration of user contexts from unstructured social media text, revealing unmet needs that can directly inform NPD (new product development) without requiring expensive surveys or focus groups. The HSCS algorithm's ability to expand concept spaces beyond direct product mentions (Pc ∩ Wi) is key to discovering latent needs.
all_findings.open_question: How does the Context Tree compare quantitatively to supervised or semi-supervised methods for needs extraction? Is the co-occurrence-based keyword selection optimal, or would embedding-based similarity improve the tree structure?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The Context Tree method will identify at least 3 novel unmet customer needs per product category that are not captured by traditional survey or FGI methods, as validated by expert evaluation of the generated user scenarios.

all_findings.source: applsci-10-08473-v2.pdf
all_findings.finding: Applied to 5 home appliances using Korean Twitter and Naver blog data (May 2012-April 2013, 100K posts per product). For 'oven', discovered contexts include: 'usually fails to cook with oven' (keyword: fail), 'more troublesome to cook with oven than microwave or gas stove' (keyword: troublesome), 'want to make healthy snacks with the oven' (keyword: snack). Expanding beyond 'oven' to related keywords (bread, snack, baking) revealed additional needs about recipe importance, preheat temperature control, and healthy snack-making for kids.
all_findings.implication: The HSCS algorithm captures both direct experiences ('oven fails') and latent expansion needs ('healthy snacks for kids without oven'), providing a more comprehensive view than keyword-frequency approaches. The social media data (100K posts) is sufficient to populate meaningful context trees.
all_findings.open_question: How does the quality of discovered contexts degrade with smaller datasets (<10K posts)? What is the minimum viable data size for effective Context Tree construction?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Context Trees built from 50K+ social media posts per product will identify at least as many actionable user scenarios as 100K-posts trees, with no significant drop in expert-rated usefulness of discovered contexts.

all_findings.source: applsci-10-08473-v2.pdf
all_findings.finding: The HSCS algorithm's key innovation is discovering 'concept spaces where primal keyword p is NOT present but related keywords are' (Pc ∩ Wi). These represent potential new use cases where the target product could satisfy unmet needs but currently does not. This expands the product's concept space from P to P', enabling identification of entirely new application domains, not just improvements to existing ones.
all_findings.implication: This distinction between P∩Wi (current usage contexts) and Pc∩Wi (potential expansion contexts) is methodologically important. Most needs-analysis methods only capture the former. The HSCS approach explicitly models product-concept expansion, which is more aligned with disruptive innovation discovery.
all_findings.open_question: How can the Pc∩Wi concept spaces be validated as genuine unmet needs rather than irrelevant associations? What methods can filter noisy or coincidental co-occurrences?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: User scenarios derived from Pc∩Wi concept spaces (product-absent contexts) will be rated by product managers as more innovative and surprising than those from P∩Wi (product-present contexts), in a blind evaluation across 5 product categories.

all_findings.source: document.pdf (Liu, 2021 - Deconstructing search tasks in IIR)
all_findings.finding: Systematic review of 76 task-based IIR studies (2000-2020) reveals 4 categories of objective task dimensions (OTDs): task complexity (n=13, most studied), task type (n=13), combined-dimensions type (n=11), and other dimensions (n=5). Of task-user combined dimensions (TUDs): topic familiarity/knowledge (n=5), subjective task difficulty (n=4), domain knowledge (n=3), and others (n=4). Research efforts are unequally distributed, with task complexity and difficulty dominating while other dimensions remain underexplored.
all_findings.implication: The field has concentrated on a narrow set of task dimensions. This creates blind spots: factors like task stage, scenario, and structure may be equally or more important for search behavior but lack sufficient empirical grounding. Future research should diversify task dimensions studied.
all_findings.open_question: Which underexplored task dimensions (task scenario, structure, stage) have the largest effect on search behavior and outcomes when systematically compared?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Task stage (early vs. late in a project) will explain more variance in query reformulation behavior than task complexity or difficulty, across a controlled experiment manipulating all three dimensions.

all_findings.source: document.pdf (Liu, 2021 - Deconstructing search tasks in IIR)
all_findings.finding: Predictors fall into 3 types: online behavioral measures (dominant), neuro-physiological measures (eye tracking, EEG, fMRI), and non-search situational measures (location, time, work tasks). No one-size-fits-all model exists. Different task dimensions require different feature sets and prediction approaches. Task prediction is domain-specific and context-dependent.
all_findings.implication: Building a universal task-aware search system is unlikely to succeed. Instead, researchers should develop context-specific predictive models matched to particular task dimensions and search scenarios. The choice of predictor features must be tailored to the specific task dimension being predicted.
all_findings.open_question: Can meta-learning or transfer learning approaches enable cross-task-dimension prediction? Is there a universal feature set that works reasonably well across all task dimensions?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A meta-learning approach trained on 5+ different task dimension prediction tasks will achieve within 10% of task-specific models on held-out task dimensions, demonstrating transferable feature representations across task types.

all_findings.source: document.pdf (Liu, 2021 - Deconstructing search tasks in IIR)
all_findings.finding: Key methodological challenges identified: (1) boundaries between task complexity categories are often unclear and not quantitatively measurable; (2) validity and reliability metrics for task typologies are rarely reported; (3) self-reported TUD measures are subject to biases and cannot be controlled beforehand; (4) many studies fail to fully report behavioral features used for prediction, hindering reproducibility.
all_findings.implication: The field needs standardized reporting guidelines for task-based IIR studies, similar to CONSORT in clinical trials. Without this, meta-analysis and systematic comparison across studies remains fundamentally limited. The lack of reliability metrics for task manipulations undermines the scientific foundation of task-based IR.
all_findings.open_question: What would a standardized reporting checklist for task-based IIR studies include, and how much would it improve replicability and comparability across studies?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A 15-item reporting checklist for task-based IIR studies will identify on average 4 missing methodological details per published paper when applied retrospectively to the 76 papers in the review dataset.

all_findings.source: li2020.pdf (Li & Ma, 2020 - Charting the Path to Purchase Using Topic Models)
all_findings.finding: Integrated CTM-HMM model connects latent topics in consumer search phrases to website visits and purchase decisions. Using data from a major hospitality firm (2,093 consumers, 40,985 visits, 8,163 search phrases), the model identifies 5 latent topics: convenience, economy, loyalty, luxury, and location. The model achieves 67.2% TPR at 5% FPR vs. 37.8% (no text), 54.9% (heuristic), and 52.7% (LDA-only).
all_findings.implication: Search phrase semantics provide substantial lift for purchase intention inference beyond behavioral signals alone. The CTM-HMM joint estimation approach outperforms two-stage pipelines (LDA first, then prediction), demonstrating that integrating topic and choice models captures richer state-dependent dynamics.
all_findings.open_question: Would the model benefit from multilingual search phrases or dynamic topic evolution? How does performance degrade with shorter observation windows or sparser search data?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The CTM-HMM model will maintain a >15% TPR advantage over a no-text baseline across 3 different product categories (hospitality, retail, finance) at 5% FPR, demonstrating cross-domain generalizability of the approach.

all_findings.source: li2020.pdf (Li & Ma, 2020 - Charting the Path to Purchase Using Topic Models)
all_findings.finding: Key behavioral insight: consumers in early purchase stages are more likely to search 'convenience' and 'loyalty' topics, while probability of searching both decreases as consumers progress toward purchase. Topic choice dynamics provide a window into latent purchase intent that clickstream data alone cannot capture.
all_findings.implication: Marketing interventions can be timed based on observed topic shifts in search queries: early-stage consumers seen searching convenience topics might respond to brand awareness campaigns, while those shifting toward specific hotel names may have higher purchase intent. This enables topic-aware targeted marketing.
all_findings.open_question: How stable are these topic-purchase-stage relationships across different time periods, seasonal contexts, and consumer segments (business vs. leisure travelers)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The 'convenience' topic (downtown, airport, city, center) will be most prevalent in early purchase stages for business travelers, while 'luxury' topic (beach, resort, spa) will show a different temporal pattern for leisure travelers, confirming that topic-purchase stage mappings are segment-specific.

all_findings.source: li2020.pdf (Li & Ma, 2020 - Charting the Path to Purchase Using Topic Models)
all_findings.finding: The model uses a modified Correlated Topic Model (CTM) within a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) framework, where topic probabilities are state-dependent (logistic transformation) and all individual-level parameters are jointly estimated from a multivariate Normal distribution. This allows topic correlations and full integration of search text with visit/purchase behavior, unlike standard LDA which assumes topic independence and two-stage estimation.
all_findings.implication: The methodological innovation — joint CTM-HMM estimation with state-dependent topic probabilities — demonstrates that search semantics and behavioral outcomes should be modeled together, not separately. The correlated topic structure (CTM over LDA) is important: topics like 'economy' and 'luxury' are negatively correlated, reflecting real tradeoffs in consumer consideration.
all_findings.open_question: How would the model perform with Transformer-based embeddings (BERT, SBERT) instead of CTM for topic extraction? Would neural topic models capture more nuanced purchase-stage signals?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A neural topic model (BERTopic or NTM) integrated into the same HMM framework would identify more fine-grained purchase-stage-specific topics than CTM, but would not significantly improve overall purchase prediction accuracy (within 2% TPR at 5% FPR).

all_findings.source: pnas.1005962107.pdf (Goel et al., 2010 - Predicting consumer behavior with Web search)
all_findings.finding: Search query volume predicts future consumer behavior across 3 domains: movies (opening weekend box office, r=0.85), video games (first-month sales, r=0.76), and music (Billboard Hot 100 rank, r=0.56). Predictive power persists weeks in advance: movie searches 4 weeks before release correlate at r=0.75 with opening weekend revenue.
all_findings.implication: Search data is a leading indicator of consumer activity, not just a contemporaneous correlate. The predictive signal exists well before the event, suggesting that pre-purchase information seeking (search) precedes and predicts purchase behavior across entertainment categories.
all_findings.open_question: How does the predictive lead time vary across categories and product types? What are the limits of search-based prediction (e.g., for infrequent purchases or low-consideration goods)?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Search-based prediction models will maintain significant predictive power (r>0.6) at lead times of 4+ weeks for high-consideration purchases (autos, electronics, travel) but will decay to r<0.4 within 1 week for low-consideration purchases (CPG, fast food).

all_findings.source: pnas.1005962107.pdf (Goel et al., 2010 - Predicting consumer behavior with Web search)
all_findings.finding: Search data is most valuable when key baseline indicators are unavailable. For movies (with available baseline: budget, screens, HSX predictions), search adds nothing to the combined model (r=0.94 baseline vs. 0.94 combined). For sequel video games (baseline: predecessor sales + critic ratings), search adds modestly (0.80 to 0.83). For non-sequel games (baseline: critic ratings only), search adds dramatically (0.44 to 0.80). For music (autoregressive), search adds significantly (0.70 to 0.87).
all_findings.implication: The value of search data is inversely related to the availability of other good predictors. Search data is most useful for new products (no prior sales data, no marketing budget estimates) and for domains where traditional prediction inputs are weak or unavailable. This suggests a 'data complementarity' rather than 'data substitution' role for search data.
all_findings.open_question: What is the marginal value of search data when combined with increasingly sophisticated ML baselines (XGBoost, neural nets)? Does the relative advantage of search shrink or persist?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: When combined with a modern ML baseline (gradient boosting with 50+ features), search query volume will improve predictive R-squared by <0.02 for movies but >0.08 for non-sequel video games, replicating the asymmetric value pattern found with simpler baselines.

all_findings.source: pnas.1005962107.pdf (Goel et al., 2010 - Predicting consumer behavior with Web search)
all_findings.finding: Re-evaluation of Google Flu Trends shows that search data's advantage over a simple autoregressive (AR) model is modest: AR model (using flu reports from 2-3 weeks prior) achieves r=0.86 vs. search-based r=0.94. If CDC reporting delay were reduced from 2 weeks to 1 week, AR model would achieve r=0.95, matching or exceeding search-based estimates.
all_findings.implication: The widely-cited Google Flu Trends results must be contextualized against appropriate baselines. Search data's perceived predictive power in epidemiology was partly due to the information lag in traditional reporting systems, not the inherent superiority of search signals. This highlights the importance of always benchmarking against simple, well-specified alternative models before claiming predictive value.
all_findings.open_question: Are there domains where search data genuinely outperforms well-specified baseline models, or is its primary value always in speed/convenience/coverage rather than accuracy?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: In at least 3 of 5 domains tested, a well-specified AR or baseline model using existing data sources will match or exceed search-based prediction accuracy, while search data's primary advantage will be in real-time availability and geographic granularity.

all_findings.source: ssrn-1340267.pdf (Kim, Albuquerque & Bronnenberg, 2009 - Online Demand Under Limited Consumer Search)
