all_findings.finding: Optimal sequential search model (Weitzman 1979 framework) integrated with random utility choice model, estimated using Amazon.com view-rank data for camcorders (91 products, 168K+ pairwise view-ranks). Median search set size is 11 products out of 90+ available (~12%). The model uses reservation utilities (zij = Vij + ζ(cij/σij)×σij) to determine which products consumers search in what order.
all_findings.implication: Consumers consider a tiny fraction of available options (~12%) even when search is nearly costless online. The sequential search model with a 'selection rule' (sort by reservation utility), 'stopping rule' (stop when best found > max remaining reservation utility), and 'choice rule' (pick best from searched set) provides a computationally tractable framework for modeling limited consideration.
all_findings.open_question: How well does the optimal sequential search model generalize to categories with different search costs and consideration set sizes? Does the reservation utility ordering hold for experience goods vs. search goods?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The optimal sequential search model will predict Amazon view-rank data with higher accuracy (lower RMSE on pairwise view-rank predictions) than a random consideration set model or a top-N heuristic across 3 different product categories (electronics, books, apparel).

all_findings.source: ssrn-1340267.pdf (Kim, Albuquerque & Bronnenberg, 2009 - Online Demand Under Limited Consumer Search)
all_findings.finding: Large majority (~70%) of product pairs are viewed together by <5% of consumers, implying severe limits on substitution and many cross-price elasticities near zero. Ten products with highest average view-list appearance frequency have significantly higher market presence. Consumer search costs are heterogeneous and significantly affected by product reference frequency (Lj) at Amazon.com.
all_findings.implication: Market structure inferred from purchase data alone may substantially overstate cross-product competition. Two products that never appear in the same consumer's search set have zero substitution regardless of attribute similarity. The view-rank data provides a revealed-preference measure of substitution that purchase-based demand models miss. Amazon's product recommendations function as search cost reducers, increasing consumer surplus.
all_findings.open_question: How stable is the view-rank-based substitution structure over time? Does it change with seasonality, new product entry, or changes in Amazon's recommendation algorithm?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The pairwise view-rank structure for camcorder products will show >80% rank correlation between May 2006 and October 2007, indicating that product co-viewership patterns are relatively stable and reflect persistent consumer search strategies rather than transient browsing behavior.

all_findings.source: ssrn-1340267.pdf (Kim, Albuquerque & Bronnenberg, 2009 - Online Demand Under Limited Consumer Search)
all_findings.finding: Model identifies heterogeneous consumer preferences and search costs from aggregate view-rank data alone, without requiring individual-level browsing histories. The key computational insight: sequential search reduces the combinatorics from 2^J possible search sets to J ordered sets at the individual level, making estimation feasible for large choice sets (J=91). The view-rank data (pairwise commonality indices CIjk) provide rich information about substitution patterns.
all_findings.implication: Publicly available Amazon view-rank data can be used to estimate demand systems without proprietary sales or browsing data, lowering barriers to market structure analysis. The model leverages 168K+ pairwise inequalities (derived from view-rank ordering) as dependent variables, providing more information than aggregate market shares alone.
all_findings.open_question: How sensitive are the demand estimates to the assumption that consumers have full information about product attributes (Vij) prior to search? What if consumers learn about products during the search process?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: A model assuming partial pre-search knowledge (consumers know brand and price but not detailed attributes) will produce similar substitution patterns to the full-information model (rank correlation >0.80) but will estimate lower mean search costs, as consumers must search more to resolve attribute uncertainty.

all_findings.source: suh2021.pdf (Suh et al., 2021 - Population-Scale Study of Human Needs During COVID-19)
all_findings.finding: Computational framework using Maslow's hierarchy of needs applied to 35+ billion Bing search interactions across 36,000+ ZIP codes (86% of US) over 14 months (Jan-Aug 2019 and 2020). Difference-in-differences methodology controls for seasonality and volume variations. Framework maps queries and clicks to 5 need categories (Physiological, Safety, Love & Belonging, Cognitive, Self Actualization) and 79 subcategories, achieving 97.2% precision.
all_findings.implication: Web search logs can be used at population scale to monitor shifts in fundamental human needs in near-real-time, providing a complement to survey-based methods. The 97% precision via regular expression detectors + click signals suggests that search behavior is a reliable signal for needs detection. The difference-in-differences approach (comparing 2020 to 2019) effectively controls for seasonal confounds.
all_findings.open_question: How does the framework's precision-recall tradeoff change for low-frequency needs (e.g., domestic violence queries are less common than health queries)? Can active learning improve detector performance for rare but critical needs?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The framework will maintain >85% precision across all 79 subcategories, but recall will vary significantly (>50% for common needs, <20% for rare needs), with rare-need recall improvable to >40% via active learning from 500 manually labeled examples per rare subcategory.

all_findings.source: suh2021.pdf (Suh et al., 2021 - Population-Scale Study of Human Needs During COVID-19)
all_findings.finding: Basic needs (Physiological, Safety) increased exponentially during the pandemic while higher-level aspirations (Self Actualization, Cognitive) declined. Top increases: toilet paper purchase (+12,691%), stimulus queries (+28,602%), unemployment queries (+5,156%), COVID-19 protection purchase (+4,634%). Top decreases: wedding purchase (-70%), wedding site visits (-66%), educational degree queries (-53%), housing queries (-53%). Physiological needs peaked ~4 weeks before Safety needs.
all_findings.implication: The pandemic caused a systematic regression down Maslow's hierarchy: attention shifted from growth and self-fulfillment to basic survival needs. The temporal lag between Physiological and Safety peaks (~4 weeks) suggests a causal sequence: health concerns → policy responses (lockdowns) → economic insecurity. This has implications for the timing and sequencing of policy interventions.
all_findings.open_question: To what extent were the shifts driven by actual changes in underlying needs vs. changes in search medium (more online activity due to lockdowns)? Can the difference-in-differences approach fully disentangle these?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The relative change in online grocery search (+380%) will correlate at r>0.8 with actual grocery e-commerce revenue data, while the decrease in job search queries (-47%) will negatively correlate with actual unemployment claims (r<-0.7), validating that search shifts reflect real behavioral changes, not just medium effects.

all_findings.source: suh2021.pdf (Suh et al., 2021 - Population-Scale Study of Human Needs During COVID-19)
all_findings.finding: Geographical analysis: states with earlier shelter-in-place mandates had significantly different short-term social-emotional response (more online social activity, less wedding interest, less mental health resource seeking) compared to later-mandate states. Longer mandate duration correlated with sustained negative mental health expression and slower recovery of wedding-related needs (Mississippi at 24 days vs. Oregon at 88 days: -33% vs. +27% negative mental health experiences).
all_findings.implication: Shelter-in-place policies have measurable, geographically varying impacts on social and emotional needs beyond their epidemiological effects. Earlier, longer mandates may reduce health outcomes (fewer COVID cases) but increase psychological costs (more negative mental health expression, less positive outlook). Policy evaluation frameworks should incorporate these broader human needs impacts.
all_findings.open_question: Can the trade-off between health benefits and psychological costs of SIP mandates be quantified in comparable units? What is the optimal duration that minimizes total social cost?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: States with moderate shelter-in-place durations (40-60 days) will show the best combined outcome: COVID mortality rates comparable to long-duration states (>60 days) but with significantly lower negative mental health expression (p<0.01) and faster recovery of Self Actualization needs.

all_findings.source: suh2021.pdf (Suh et al., 2021 - Population-Scale Study of Human Needs During COVID-19)
all_findings.finding: Gap analysis between expressed needs (search) and reported need fulfillment: unemployment needs from search align closely with official US DOL claims (r=0.996) but remain at 25% higher levels from April onwards, suggesting barriers to accessing benefits. Domestic violence queries dropped 37% despite expected increases during lockdowns, suggesting underreporting due to limited safe access to resources.
all_findings.implication: Search data can identify gaps between expressed needs and realized support — people searching for unemployment benefits more than claims being filed suggests confusion, denial, or difficulty accessing the system. The domestic violence decline (despite expected increase) flags a critical issue: victims may lack private access to search, not that need has decreased. These are actionable monitoring signals for policymakers.
all_findings.open_question: What specific barriers drive the 25% gap between unemployment need expression and claims filing? Are these informational (don't know how), procedural (can't navigate system), or eligibility (don't qualify) barriers?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Semantic analysis of unemployment-related search queries will reveal that the gap is primarily driven by confusion-type queries ('how to file', 'am I eligible', 'denied') rather than information-seeking about general benefits, suggesting informational/procedural barriers over eligibility ones.

all_findings.source: sustainability-13-00391-v2.pdf (Nam et al., 2021 - Identifying Service Opportunities Based on ODI and Deep Learning)
all_findings.finding: Data-driven Outcome-Driven Innovation (ODI) framework using BERT-based deep learning to identify service opportunities from online reviews. Pipeline: (1) sentence classification to Service Job Map steps via semantic similarity-binary relevance (SS-BR) using BERT, (2) SAO (subject-action-object) extraction via syntactic analysis, (3) SAO-to-outcome transformation via BERT-based metric similarity, (4) opportunity scoring via importance-satisfaction gap (SOS = Importance + max(Importance - Satisfaction, 0)).
all_findings.implication: This bridges expert-driven ODI methodology (typically requiring 100+ customer interviews) with automated text analysis, making ODI more scalable and less dependent on expert judgment. The SS-BR approach (77.05% accuracy) outperforms traditional TDNN (72.35%) by providing BERT with semantic step descriptions as context, leveraging sentence-pair classification capabilities of pre-trained language models.
all_findings.open_question: How well does the automated ODI pipeline replicate the results of expert-conducted ODI interviews for the same service? What is the agreement rate between automated and expert-derived opportunity scores?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Automated ODI pipeline (using the described method) will identify at least 60% of the same top-10 service opportunities as expert-conducted ODI interviews in a hotel service context, demonstrating practical utility as a screening tool.

all_findings.source: sustainability-13-00391-v2.pdf (Nam et al., 2021 - Identifying Service Opportunities Based on ODI and Deep Learning)
all_findings.finding: Empirical study on hotel service (385 Trip Advisor reviews, Royal Plaza Hotel): 1,057 SAO structures extracted and clustered into customer outcomes. Seven high-opportunity outcomes identified (SOS ≥ 10): minimize cost to check in early (SOS=14.0), maximize safety to pay price/deposit (SOS=14.0), maximize possibility to avoid lobby waiting (SOS=12.5), maximize possibility of preferred room assignment (SOS=12.0), maximize possibility to avoid billing mistakes (SOS=12.0), minimize cost to book (SOS=10.5), minimize effort to check prices (SOS=10.0).
all_findings.implication: The most important hotel service opportunities (SOS≥14) relate to financial/payment trust (safety of payment, early check-in cost) rather than service features or amenities. This suggests that hotel innovation priorities should focus on transactional trust and flexibility rather than luxury additions. Steps 5 (initial service delivery) and 10 (problem resolution) of the Service Job Map generate the most outcomes.
all_findings.open_question: How do the identified opportunities vary across hotel segments (budget, mid-scale, luxury)? Would different data sources (Yelp, Google Reviews) or different hotels yield different priority outcomes?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: The top-3 service opportunities (early check-in cost, payment safety, waiting reduction) will appear across all hotel segments, but their relative SOS ranking will differ: budget hotels will rank 'minimize cost to book' higher, while luxury hotels will rank 'maximize possibility of preferred room assignment' higher.

all_findings.source: sustainability-13-00391-v2.pdf (Nam et al., 2021 - Identifying Service Opportunities Based on ODI and Deep Learning)
all_findings.finding: Technical innovation: SS-BR (semantic similarity-binary relevance) method for multi-class sentence classification uses BERT to compute semantic similarity between each review sentence and 13 semantic descriptions (12 job map steps + 1 noise class). The sentence is assigned to the step with highest similarity. This outperforms standard TDNN (one-vs-all) across all test runs, with average 77.05% vs. 72.35% accuracy, and converges faster (<2 epochs vs. >5 epochs).
all_findings.implication: Leveraging pre-trained BERT's sentence-pair classification capability via semantic descriptions is superior to fine-tuning BERT as a standalone classifier for domain-specific taxonomies. The external semantic descriptions provide richer signal than implicit category learning. The fast convergence (<2 epochs) suggests the method is sample-efficient and suitable for small datasets.
all_findings.open_question: How sensitive is SS-BR performance to the quality of the semantic descriptions? Would automatically generated descriptions (from category keyword summaries) work as well as manually written ones?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: SS-BR accuracy will degrade by <5% when semantic descriptions are replaced with automatically generated summaries (extractive from category exemplars) compared to manually written descriptions, suggesting the method is robust to description quality.

all_findings.source: sustainability-13-00391-v2.pdf (Nam et al., 2021 - Identifying Service Opportunities Based on ODI and Deep Learning)
all_findings.finding: The Service Job Map (12 steps) provides finer granularity than the Universal Job Map (8 steps) for service process analysis. Steps 5 (Initial service delivery, 28 outcomes) and 10 (Get questions answered / problems resolved, 34 outcomes) generate the most representative outcomes, while Step 9 (Adjust service plan, 3 outcomes) and Step 11 (Conclude service, 4 outcomes) generate the fewest.
all_findings.implication: The distribution of customer needs across the service process is highly uneven: check-in and problem-resolution are the most pain-point-rich stages, while service adjustment and conclusion are relatively pain-free. This suggests where service design efforts should be concentrated and provides a quantitative basis for resource allocation in service innovation.
all_findings.open_question: Does the distribution of outcomes across Service Job Map steps generalize to other service types (healthcare, retail, transportation)? Is the concentration in steps 5 and 10 specific to hospitality?
all_findings.testable_hypothesis: Across 5 different service types, steps 5 and 10 will consistently contain >40% of all identified outcomes, while steps 9 and 11 will consistently contain <10%, suggesting a universal pattern in customer pain-point distribution across service processes.
